ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE REGULAR SESSION OF THE GENERAL ASSEM-
BLY , WHICH WAS BEGUN IN THE CITY OF FRANKFORT , KEN-
TUCKY , ON TUESDAY , JANUARY THE EIGHTH , 1946 , AND
ENDED THURSDAY , MARCH THE TWENTY -FIRST , 1946 .
CHAPTER 1
(H. B. 20)
AN ACT establishing and regulating the maximum hours and maximum
days members of the Police Department in Cities of the Second Class
shall work .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That in all cities of the Second Class within
the Commonwealth of Kentucky , except those in which by
referendum ordinance , the patrolmen are employed or paid
by the day , the members of the Police Department shall not
be required to work more than eight ( 8 ) hours per day for
six (6) days each week , except in the event of an emergency .
Section 2. Each member of the Police Department in
such cities of the Second Class shall have an annual leave of
fifteen ( 15 ) days with full pay.
Section 3. All laws or parts of laws in conflict herewith
are hereby repealed .
Passed February 21 , enrolled February 26 , approved March 7 , 1946 .
CHAPTER 2
(H. B. 24)
AN ACT to amend Section 183 of the Criminal Code of Practice .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 183 of the Criminal Code of Practice is amended
to read as follows :
If the indictment be for a felony , the defendant must be
present , and , if on bail , may remain on bail during the trial ,
unless the court, which it may do in its discretion , shall require

Passed and enrolled March 21 , approved March 23 , 1946 .
CHAPTER 3
(H. B. 26 )
AN ACT providing for the disposition of sums assigned to the State
Treasurer by state officers and employes for the purpose of purchasing
bonds of the United States Government , and subsequently abandoned .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
( 1 ) Where any officer or employe of the state govern-
ment or of any agency of the state government has authorized
the State Treasurer to deduct from his compensation as such
officer or employe a sum or sums for the purchase of United
States Series E Savings Bonds , and thereafter , for any
cause , has departed from such office or employment leaving
unclaimed in the hands of the State Treasurer a sum arising
from such deduction not equal to the amount for which such
a bond may be purchased , the State Treasurer shall , within
ninety days after the date of the last deduction , mail to such
officer or employe , at his last-known address as shown on the
records of the Division of Personnel Efficiency , a notice stat-
ing the sum held by the State Treasurer for such officer or
employe , and requesting that he make claim for the same with-
in six months thereafter . A duplicate of such notice , ad-
dressed to the officer or employe , shall at the same time be
delivered to the state agency of which the person was an
officer or employe . If, at the expiration of six months from the
date of mailing the letter , the officer or employe has not made
claim for the sum due him , the sum shall , as of July 1 following
the expiration of such six-months ' period , be presumed
abandoned .
( 2 ) On or before September 1 of each year the State
Treasurer shall report to the Department of Revenue , in
duplicate , a list of the sums presumed to be abandoned as of

CHAPTER 4
(H. B. 29 )
AN ACT relating to the preservation and disposal of cancelled checks in
the hands of the State Treasurer .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Checks of the State Treasurer upon state depository
banks shall , after cancellation , be preserved by the State
Treasurer for a period of seven years . At the expiration of
that period , unless required for the purpose of an action
then pending , the checks shall be sold as waste paper or
destroyed .
Passed February 5 , enrolled February 6 , approved February 18 , 1946 .

(H. B. 30)

President of the United States or by the Governor as days of
thanksgiving , are holidays , on which all the public offices of
this Commonwealth may be closed ; and shall be considered as
Sunday for all purposes regarding the presenting for pay-
ment or acceptance , and of protesting for and giving notice
of the dishonor of bills of exchange , bank checks and promis-
sory notes , placed by law upon the footing of bills of exchange .
If any of the days named as holidays occur on Sunday, the
next day thereafter shall be observed as a holiday but bills
of exchange or other papers may be presented for payment or
acceptance on the Saturday preceding the holiday and pro-
ceeded on accordingly .
(2) No person shall be compelled to labor on the first
Monday in September (Labor Day ) by any person .
§ 2. Whereas , a proper respect for the memory of our
late President requires that provisions be made immediately
for perpetuating his memory , an emergency is declared to
exist and this Act shall take effect upon its passage and ap-
proval by the Governor .
Passed January 28 , enrolled January 29 , vetoed February 6 , passed
over veto February 14 , 1946 .

6 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
is in danger from fires . He shall report his proceedings to
the city legislative body when required .
( 2 ) The chief shall direct and control the operations of
the members of the fire department in the discharge of their
duties . He shall have access to and use of all cisterns , fire-
plugs , the waters of the waterworks and the cisterns of private
persons , for the purpose of extinguishing fires . He shall have
the right to examine all cisterns , and all plugs and pipes of
the waterworks , to see that they are in condition for use in
case of fire . He shall have control of all buildings , hose , en-
gines and other equipment provided for the fire department .
He shall perform such other duties as the city legislative body
shall, by ordinance prescribe .
(3) The fire department of each second class city shall
be divided into not less than two platoons , one of which
platoons shall be on duty at a time but neither platoon shall
be on duty more than twenty -four consecutive hours , except
in cases of extraordinary emergency , after which the platoon
serving twenty -four hours shall be allowed to remain off duty
for at least twenty -four consecutive hours . Each individual
member of the platoons in addition to receiving a minimum
of twenty -four hours off duty in each period of forty- eight
hours shall receive an additional period of twenty -four con-
secutive hours off duty in each period of fourteen days . The
chief of the fire department shall arrange the schedule of
working hours to comply with the provisions of this section .
(4) In each city of the second class all employes of the
fire department shall be given not less than two weeks leave
of absence annually , with full pay.
Passed February 15 , enrolled February 20 , approved February 21 ,
1946 .
CHAPTER 8
(H. B. 39)
AN ACT relating to Cities of the Fourth Class .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
That subsection ( 3 ) of section 86.050 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(3) The city council may , by ordinance , allow each mem-
ber of the next council not more than five dollars for each
meeting of the council he attends .
Passed February 18 , enrolled February 20 , approved February 21 ,
1946 .

Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context re-
quires otherwise :
( 1 ) " Gasoline is defined as in KRS 138.210 ( 4 ) .
(2 ) " Dealer " is defined as in KRS 138.210 ( 2 ) .
(3) " Department " means the Department of Revenue .
(4) Agricultural purposes " means purposes directly
related to the production of agricultural commodities and the
conducting of ordinary activities on the farm .
( 5 ) " Public highways " means every way or place gen-
erally open to the use of the public as a matter of right for
the purpose of vehicular travel .
Section 2. Any person who shall use any gasoline , other
than on public highways , on which the tax as imposed by KRS
138.220 has been paid , for the purpose of operating or pro-
pelling stationary gasoline engines or tractors for agricultural
purposes , shall be reimbursed to the extent of ninety per
cent of the amount of the tax so paid on such gasoline ;
provided , however , that no refund shall be authorized unless
applications therefor and all necessary information shall be
filed with the Department of Revenue within one hundred and
twenty days from the date of purchase ; and further provided
that no refund shall be authorized for purchases of less than
fifty gallons at any one time ; and further provided that no
refund shall be authorized unless the amount due is as much
as five dollars .
Section 3. No person shall secure a refund of tax under
section two of this Act unless such person is the holder of an
unrevoked refund permit issued by the Department of Reve-
nue before the purchase of the gasoline , which permit shall
entitle such person to make application for a refund under
this Act . To procure a permit every such person shall file
with the department an application under oath , on forms
furnished by the department , setting forth such informaion
incident to the refunding of the tax paid on gasoline as the
department may require . Such application shall be filed with
the department at least fifteen days before the date when the
permit is to become effective .
Section 4. The department may , if the applicant for a
refund permit has violated any provision of this Act or regu-
lation pursuant thereto , or has been convicted of bribery ,
theft or false swearing within the period of five years preced-
ing the application , or if the department has evidence of the

CHAPTER 10 13
operation of this Act or the enforcement thereof ; provided ,
however , that no agent shall enter the dwelling of any person
without the occupant's consent or the authority from a court

Section 7. No person shall execute a gasoline refund
invoice , as described in section eight of this Act, who is not
a dealer , as defined in KRS 138.210 ( 2 ) or a sub-jobber duly
authorized by a licensed dealer , to execute gasoline refund
invoices as his agent . In no instance shall refund invoices
be executed for purchases from retail filling stations .
Section 8. (1 ) When gasoline is sold to a person who
shall claim to be entitled to refund under section two of this
Act , the seller of such gasoline shall make out in duplicate ,
with double back carbon , a gasoline refund invoice supplied
by the department , which invoice shall have printed thereon
that the liability to the Commonwealth of Kentucky for the
excise tax imposed under KRS 138.220 with respect to such
gasoline has been assumed by the seller and that said excise
tax has already been paid or will be paid by the seller when
the same shall become payable , a statement setting forth the
name and address of the purchaser , the number of gallons of
gasoline so sold , the proposed use for which such gasoline is
purchased , and such other information as the department
shall require . The original of such gasoline refund invoice
shall be given to the purchaser and the duplicate shall be
retained by the seller .
(2) The refund permit holder shall file with the depart-
ment an application for refund on forms furnished by the
department , stating the quantity of gasoline used for the
purposes as set out in section two of this Act. Such applica-
tion shall be accompanied by the original invoice , or certified
copy thereof , showing such purchase , and , if required by the
department , evidence of payment therefor . When the depart-
ment is satisfied that a refund is proper , it shall authorize
ninety per cent of the amount of tax paid to be refunded as
other refunds are made ; and the amount refunded shall be
deducted from gasoline tax receipts .
(3) The right to receive any refund under the provisions
of this section shall not be assignable , except to the executor
or administrator , or to the receiver , trustee in bankruptcy , or
assignee in insolvency proceedings of such person entitled
thereto .

Section 9. Upon the refusal of the department to au-
thorize a refund , the applicant may in the manner and subject
to the conditions provided in KRS 131.110 ( 1 ) petition for a
review thereof by the Kentucky Tax Commission ; and if the
applicant is not satisfied with the decision of the commission ,
he may appeal to the Franklin circuit court in the manner
and subject to the conditions provided in KRS 131.120 .
Section 10. If any excise taxes on gasoline be erroneous-
ly refunded , the department shall advise the payee by reg-
istered mail of the erroneous refund . If the payee fails to
reimburse the Commonwealth within fifteen days ater the
receipt of the letter , an action may be instituted by the depart-
ment in the Franklin circuit court and the department shall
recover from the payee the amount of the erroneous refund
plus a penalty of twenty per cent .
Section 11. ( 1 ) No person shall make a false or fraudu-
lent statement in an application for a refund permit or in a
gasoline refund invoice , or in an application for a refund of
any taxes under this Act ; or fraudulently obtain a refund of
such taxes ; or knowingly aid or assist in making any such
false or fraudulent statement or claim ; or having bought
gasoline under the provisions of this Act , shall use or permit
such gasoline or any part thereof to be used for any purpose
other than as provided in section two of this Act .
( 2 ) The refund permit of any person who shall violate
any provision of subsection ( 1 ) of this section shall be revoked
by the Kentucky Tax Commission , and may not be reissued
until two years have elapsed from the date of such revocation ,
and such person , whether or not his permit has been revoked
by the commission , shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and upon
conviction thereof , shall be fined not less than one hundred
dollars nor more than five hundred dollars , or imprisoned not
exceeding one year , or both in the discretion of the court .
(3) The refund permit of any person who shall violate
any provision of this Act , other than those contained in sub-
section ( 1 ) of this section , may be suspended by the Kentucky
Tax Commission for any period in its discretion not exceed-
ing six months .
(4 ) If a dealer violates any provision of this Act, his
privilege to sign gasoline refund invoices may be suspended
by the Kentucky Tax Commission for a period of not more

CHAPTER 12 17
CHAPTER 11
(H. B. 55 )
AN ACT relating to trial commissioners for county courts .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 25.280 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes of 1944
is amended to read as follows :
The judge of the county court , in each county of this
Commonwealth wherein the fiscal court may authorize , is
hereby authorized to appoint a trial commissioner or com-
missioners who shall hold office at the pleasure of the judge ,
and shall discharge such judicial duties as may be assigned
to him by the judge . The commissioner shall report his ac-
tions to the judge for the approval or disapproval of the
judge . The commissioner must possess the qualifications of
a judge of the county court . He shall receive a salary to be
fixed by the fiscal court of the county , which salary shall not
exceed fifty dollars per annum , except in counties containing
a population of two hundred thousand or more and in
counties containing a city of the second class , in which
counties , by reason of the increased duties of the county
judge resulting from the amount of population , the fiscal
court may fix the salary of the commissioner at not more than
thirty-six hundred dollars per annum . The salary of the
commissioner shall be paid in the same way as the county
judge is paid .
Passed February 5 , enrolled February 7 , approved February 8 , 1946 .
CHAPTER 12
(H. B. 56 )
AN ACT relating to county boards of supervisors of tax .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 133.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
(1 ) The county board of supervisors shall be composed
of reputable property owners residing in the county , to be
appointed by the county judge annually at the January term .
The board shall consist of three members appointed from the
county at large , unless the fiscal court shall by order direct
that the board be composed of one member from each magis-

classification.title: AN ACT making it an offense to board or lodge children under the age of sixteen years, or in any manner to conduct a boarding house or boarding home for children under the age of sixteen years, without being authorized and empowered so to do, in writing, by the Health Officer of the county in which such boarding house or boarding home is conducted; providing for investigation to be made by the Health Officer of such county; providing for the revocation of such power and authority and providing a limitation upon the time the same shall exist; providing that persons conducting boarding houses or boarding homes for children under the age of sixteen years shall keep a book in which facts concerning children boarded therein shall be entered; providing for an investigation of said book and the entries therein by the health officer or juvenile court of the county or any of its probation officers; providing that the county judge of any county may require that security be furnished so that no non-resident child shall become a charge upon the Commonwealth or any of its subdivisions; and providing penalties for the violation of any of the provisions of this act.

classification.title: An Act relating to the regulation of boarding homes and boarding houses for children under sixteen years of age.
classification.summary: This act requires health officers to investigate and authorize individuals or entities wishing to operate boarding homes or boarding houses for children under sixteen years of age. It mandates that these operators maintain detailed records of each child, including their name, sex, race, parents' information, placement date, birth date, and compensation. The health officer has the discretion to revoke permits if arrangements are deemed unsuitable for the children's health. Failure to obtain a permit is an offense.

While it mandates the recording of a child's 'race', this is in the context of general demographic information for regulatory purposes, not for establishing segregation or discriminatory practices. There is no indication of differential treatment based on race, nor any explicit or implicit racial restrictions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. § 3. Whenever the County Judge of any county , in his
discretion , deems it necessary that a person accepting a non-
resident child for boarding or lodging in his commonwealth ,
shall satisfy him that such a non -resident child shall never
become a charge upon the commonwealth or any subdivision
thereof, he may require that the proper public authorities of
the state or county in which non -resident child resides , fur-
nish security, to his satisfaction , that they will assume the
care and custody of such child in the event such child becomes
a charge upon this commonwealth thereof or any subdivision
thereof . Whenever any County Judge shall enter an order
that such security shall be furnished for all non -resident
children boarded or lodged in his county , no child shall there-
after be accepted for boarding or lodging in said county until
such security is furnished . Any person who accepts a non-
resident child under the age of sixteen years for boarding or
lodging , in any county , without the furnishing of the security
provided for herein , after the county judge of the county has
provided by order for the furnishing of such security , shall be
guilty of an offense and punished as hereafter provided .
§ 4. The provisions of this Act shall not affect or apply
to boarding homes in which children under the care , custody
or control of or receiving aid from the Department of Wel-
fare are being boarded , and which have been approved by the
Department of Welfare as meeting the standards of that
department for placement , nor shall the provisions of this
Act affect persons caring for and providing for children re-
lated to them by blood or marriage .
§ 5. Any person violating any of the provisions of this
Act shall be guilty of an offense , and upon conviction thereof,
shall be fined not more than five hundred dollars or imprisoned
for not more than twelve months , or be both fined and impris-
oned , in the discretion of the court .

CHAPTER 14
(H. B. 60 )
AN ACT to amend and reenact Section 281.190 , Subsection ( 3 ) Ken-
tucky Revised Statutes .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
(1 ) That Section 281.190 , Subsection ( 3 ) of the Ken-
tucky Revised Statutes be and the same is hereby amended
and reenacted so that when amended and reenacted the same
will read as follows :
If any person holding a certificate over any public high-
way in this state between fixed termini or over a regular
route fails to begin operation under the certificate on said
line for a period of sixty days , or abandons operation , there-
under, or fails to pay the renewal fees therefor for a period
of thirty days after the due date thereof , or fails to keep
the required insurance on file and in force , such certificate
shall be and become null and void , and an order to that affect
shall forthwith be entered on the records of the Division of
Motor Transportation . Provided , however , that the Division
of Motor Transportation may , for good cause shown , grant
stays of operation authorizing any person holding a certifi-
cate to begin operations under the certificate after the expira-
tion of the sixty day period or to abandon such operations
temporarily or to extend the time for payment of fees , and
provided , further , no certificate shall be revoked without
notice to the certificate holder and without a hearing pursuant
to the provisions of KRS 281.130 .
Where the holder of any such certificate over which
operations have not been commenced or have been suspended
fails or refuses at any time there is a need for services over
such certificated route , and the failure to provide such serv-
ice is not beyond the control of the certificate holder , then
upon application the Director of Motor Transportation
may grant a certificate to another applicant and may cancel
the certificate of the holder .
Passed March 12 , enrolled March 14 , approved March 22 , 1946 .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1946, p. 38

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and reenact Section 281.190, Subsection (3) Kentucky Revised Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 281.190(3) to outline conditions under which a certificate for public highway operation can become null and void. These conditions include failure to begin operation within sixty days, abandonment of operation, failure to pay renewal fees within thirty days of the due date, or failure to maintain required insurance. The Division of Motor Transportation may grant stays for good cause and no certificate shall be revoked without notice and a hearing. The Director of Motor Transportation may grant a certificate to another applicant and cancel the existing one if the holder fails to provide service when needed and the failure is not beyond their control.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of public highway transportation certificates in Kentucky. It details the conditions for certificate revocation, renewal fee payments, and operational requirements. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute applies to all certificate holders regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

AN ACT relating to Motor Vehicles , Motor Trucks and Semi -Trailer
Trucks , and Motor Vehicle Fees .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
( One ) A new section of the statutes is hereby created
to be numbered KRS 189.235 and to read as follows :
The Department in respect to State and Federal High-
ways by orders duly issued and published as directed in
K.R.S. 13.020 may increase on designated highways or por-
tions thereof , the height , length and gross weight prescribed
in K.R.S. 189.180 and K.R.S. 189.220 on any motor truck or
semi -trailer truck , if in the opinion of the Commissioner of
Highways the increased height , length and weight designated
by him is justified by the strength , safety and durability of
the designated highways and said highways do not appear
susceptible to unreasonable or unusual damage by reason of
such increases . However , in no event shall any motor truck
or semi -trailer truck , including any part of the body or load ,
exceed in height 12 feet , and , provided further , that no motor
truck , including any part of the body or load , shall exceed in
length 35 feet , and , provided further , that no semi -trailer
truck , including any part of the body or load , shall exceed in
length 45 feet . The gross weight, including load, of any
motor truck or semi -trailer truck shall not exceed 18,000
pounds per axle ( axles less than 42 inches apart shall be
considered as one axle ) nor exceed 600 pounds per inch of
aggregate width of all tires upon which such vehicles may be
propelled , and in no event shall the gross weight , including
load, exceed 42,000 pounds .
(two ) Section 186.030 K.R.S. is amended as follows :
The application blank shall contain blank spaces for , and

number , if any , model and year model , motive power , style
body , and if the motor vehicle is a truck designed for the
transportation of property , the gross weight in pounds . The
declared gross weight shall be the weight of the vehicle and
the heaviest load that the vehicle will be used to carry at any

classification.title: AN ACT relating to Motor Vehicles, Motor Trucks and Semi-Trailer Trucks, and Motor Vehicle Fees.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) by creating KRS 189.235 and amending KRS 186.030. KRS 189.235 allows the Department of Highways to increase height, length, and gross weight limits for motor trucks and semi-trailer trucks on designated highways, provided these increases are justified by highway strength and safety, and do not cause unreasonable damage. It sets specific maximum limits for height (12 feet), length for motor trucks (35 feet) and semi-trailer trucks (45 feet), and gross weight per axle (18,000 pounds) and overall (42,000 pounds). KRS 186.030 is amended to require applicants for vehicle registration to provide detailed information, including the declared gross weight for trucks used for property transportation.

classification.title: Amendments to K.R.S. Sections 189.260 and 281.480
classification.summary: This statute amends Kentucky Revised Statutes (K.R.S.) sections 189.260 and 281.480. Section 189.260 is amended to require motor vehicles licensed under K.R.S. 186.050(3) and 186.055 to prominently display their registered gross maximum weight on each side. Section 281.480 is amended to require common and contract carriers primarily transporting property for hire to pay an annual excise tax based on declared gross weight for the privilege of using public highways, in addition to the basic registration fee. The tax is due on or before January 1st of each year.

CHAPTER 16
(H. B. 67)
AN ACT amending section 74.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes ; pro-
viding for the county attorney to act as legal counsel for water dis-
tricts ; and for employment and compensation of additional counsel
for such districts .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 74.030 of Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows :
1. The County Attorney shall act as legal advisor and
counsel to the water Commission . He shall file and attend to
all suits for the collection of water assessments , for the col-
lection of lateral assessments , claims for water furnished and
all other legal business connected with the Commission .
2. The Commission shall have authority to employ ad-
ditional counsel , if required , subject to the joint approval of
the County Judge and County Attorney . Such counsel shall be
compensated from Water District Funds .
Passed February 14 , enrolled February 18 , 1946 , not approved.

vised Statutes so as to increase the amounts that may be paid as

exceeding one hundred thousand as determined by the last preceding
Federal census .

Section 29.060 is amended , so that when amended and re-

" 29.060 . COMPENSATION OF COMMISSIONERS .
Each jury commissioner shall receive for his services three
dollars per day for the time actually engaged , the claim for
which shall be certified by the judge appointing him and paid
by the State Treasurer upon the warrant of the Department of
Finance . The fiscal court of any county containing a city of the
first class may supplement the compensation of the commis-
sioners by allowing and paying to each commissioner not ex-
ceeding seven dollars per day for the time actually engaged .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :.
Section 29.050 is hereby amended by adding the following
to Subsection 2 :
" Except that in counties containing cities of the first
class , the compensation of the typist shall be two hundred
dollars per year .'
So that said Sub-section 2 of Section 29.050 , when
amended and re - enacted , shall read as follows :
"(2) The commissioners shall meet at such time ,
prompty after their appointment , as will enable them to com-
plete their duties not less than five days before the date speci-
fied by KRS 29.130 for drawing jury lists for the next term of
court, or, in the case of courts of continuous session , not less
than five days before the first Monday of the month following
the month in which they are appointed . The commissioners
shall remain in session not longer than five days , except that
in counties having a population of more than fifty thousand ,
they may remain in session ten days . They shall hold their
meetings in a room designated by the judge . While engaged in
making lists of juries , selecting the names and writing , depos-
iting or drawing them from the drum , no person shall be per-
mitted in the room with them except that the jury commission-
ers of courts of continuous session may select a typist to aid
them in the performance of their duty, who shall be sworn in
open court to faithfully discharge his duties and who shall be
in attendance on the commissioners in session . Such typist
shall be compensated at the same rate as the commissioners ,
and his compensation shall be allowed and paid by the fiscal
court, except that in counties containing cities of the first
class , the compensation of the typist shall be two hundred dol-
lars per year."

Cause correct accounts and records to be kept of all re-

CHAPTER 22
AN ACT relating to newsboys .
(H. B. 86 )
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 434.490 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
(1 ) Any person who obtains newspapers from a person
engaged in selling newspapers at retail , without paying there-
for, with intent to defraud such person selling newspapers ,
shall be fined not more than twenty -five dollars or imprisoned
not more than thirty days , or both .
( 2 ) Proof that any person refused or neglected to pay
for such newspapers upon demand shall be prima facie evi-
dence of the fraudulent intent comtemplated by this section .
Passed March 13, enrolled March 15 , approved March 22 , 1946 .

the medical committee to include a surgeon and neurologist ; and
providing for a subsequent injury fund tax and the methods for levy-
ing and collecting same , and the maintenance of a subsequent injury
fund .

CHAPTER 23 31
WHEREAS , There are now in Kentucky many disabled veterans and in
the future there will be many more , and
WHEREAS , There are also residing in this Commonwealth persons other
than veterans , who are physically handicapped , and
WHEREAS , Under the provisions of the Workmen's Compensation Law of
Kentucky such disabled veterans and other persons physically handi-
capped are placed at a disadvantage in securing employment , because
any employer hiring such persons assumes an additional liability ,
which has resulted in a reluctance in employing such persons , and
WHEREAS , It is imperative , in order to secure for such disabled persons
an equitable opportunity for employment , that the amendments pro-
posed herein be enacted into law, thus removing from the Kentucky
Workmen's Compensation Law those provisions which handicap the
employment of disabled veterans and other physically impaired per-
Therefore ,sons ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
SECTION I. Repeal and re - enact Section 342.120 , Ken-
tucky Revised Statutes , so that said section when re -enacted
shall read as follows :
If any employee who is permanently partially disabled
receives a subsequent compensable injury by accident result-
ing in additional permanent disability so that the degree of
disability caused by the combined disabilities is greater than
that which would have resulted from the subsequent injury
alone , and such employee is entitled to receive compensation
on the basis of the combined disabilities , the employer shall be
liable only for the degree of disability which would have re-
sulted from the latter injury had there been no pre-existing
disability . After the compensation liability of the employer , or
his insurance carrier , if any , has been fully discharged , the re-
maining compensation to which such resulting condition would
entitle the employee , less all compensation which the provi-
sions of this law would have afforded on account of the prior
disability had it been compensated for thereunder , shall be
paid out of the Subsequent Injury Fund provided for in Sec-
tion 4 hereof.
The term " Permanent partial disability " under the pro-
visions of this section shall not include disease or pre -existing
disease , except where the disease or pre -existing disease was
the natural and direct result of a compensable injury , or the
disease or pre -existing disease was contracted by the employee

classification.summary: This act amends Section 342.120 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning workmen's compensation. The definition of 'permanent partial disability' is also clarified.

ability resulting from a subsequent injury any medical ques-
tion shall be in controversy , or a determination thereof neces-
sary to a proper apportionment of liability therefor , the Board
shall refer the case to a panel of not less than two members of

for investigation and report . No award shall be made in any
such case until the panel of the Committee shall have investi-
gated the case and made its report with respect to all medical
questions at issue or necessary to such apportionment . The
date of disablement , if in dispute , shall be deemed a medical
question . All provisions of sub - sections ( 10 ) , ( 11 ) and ( 12 )
of Section 342.316 , relating to examinations and findings by
the Medical Committee and proceedings with respect thereto
shall apply also to examinations and findings by a panel of
the Medical Committee in subsequent injury cases .
SECTION III . Repeal and Reenact Section 342.315 ,
Kentucky Revised Statutes , so that said section when so
amended and reenacted shall read as follows :
( 1 ) The Board , or any member thereof, may upon the
application of either party , or upon its own motion , appoint a
disinterested and duly qualified physician or surgeon to make
any necessary medical examination of the employee and to
testify in respect thereto . The physician or surgeon shall be
allowed a reasonable fee to be fixed by the Board , not exceed-
ing fifteen dollars for each examination and report , except
that the Board may allow additional reasonable amounts in
extraordinary cases . The Board may allow traveling expenses
to the employee or to the physician or surgeon . The traveling
expenses and fees allowed under this sub - section shall be paid
out of the maintenance fund .

proval of the Governor , shall appoint a medical committee
composed of five disinterested and duly qualified physicians ,
including one surgeon , one neurologist, and three others who
are experienced in the diagnosis and/or treatment of occupa-
tional diseases , including silicosis , two of whom shall be radi-
ologists , whose headquarters shall be in the City of Louisville ;
said Medical Committee shall be appointed for terms of four

SECTION IV . In addition to the taxes and assessments
imposed by Kentucky Revised States 342.450 and 342.475 , a
Subsequent Injury Fund Tax at the rate of ¾ of one per cent
of the amount of premiums received shall be paid by every in-
surance carrier as defined in Kentucky Revised Statutes
342.445 subject to the credits therein set out , and the Board
shall assess against the payrolls of every employer carrying
his own risk a Subsequent Injury Fund Tax computed by tak-
ing ¾ of one per cent of the basic premiums determined as
provided in Kentucky Revised Statutes 342.475 . The pro-
visions of Kentucky Revised Statutes 342.450 , 342.455 , 342.-
460 , 342.465 and 342.470 shall , so far as applicable also apply
to the levy and collection of such additional tax of ¾ of one
per cent of such premiums of insurance carriers .
SECTION V. All taxes and assessments imposed by
Section 4 hereof shall be paid into the State Treasury and
shall be credited to the Subsequent Injury Fund for the Board ,
which fund shall be used only for the payment of awards of
compensation made by the Board and chargeable against said
fund and fees and expenses of the Medical Committee in con-
nection therewith , and shall be withdrawn for that purpose
under the supervision of the Commissioner of Industrial Re-
lations .
SECTION VI . Any sum remaining in the Subsequent
Injury Fund to the credit of the Workmen's Compensation
Board at the end of any fiscal year shall be set up by the the
Department of Finance to the credit of the Board in such fund
for the next ensuing fiscal year.
SECTION VII . If the amount to the credit of the Sub-
sequent Injury Fund as of June 30th of any year exceeds
$75,000.00 , the tax of 34 of one per cent upon insurance prem-
iums provided for under Section 4 hereof and the 34 of one per
cent assessment upon employers carrying own risk provided
for under Section 4 hereof shall not be assessed or collected
during the ensuing year .
SECTION VIII . All laws or parts of laws in conflict
herewith are hereby repealed .

CHAPTER 24 35
CHAPTER 24
(H. B. 92 )
AN ACT relating to the issuance of revenue bonds for market places
by cities of the third class , either alone or by joint action with the
county in which the city is located.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. As used in this Act , " market place " means any
building , grounds or premises suitable for and intended for
use as a place for the marketing , by the producer , of agri-
cultural and dairy products , including livestock , meats , fruit ,
poultry and eggs , and food products manufactured by the
producer in his own home .
§ 2. ( 1 ) For the purpose of defraying the cost of ac-
quiring a market place , either by purchase or construction , a
city of the third class may borrow money and issue negotiable
bonds , but only after an ordinance has been adopted by the
legislative body of the city specifying the proposed undertak-
ing, the amount of bonds to be issued , and the maximum rate
of interest the bonds are to bear . The ordinance shall further
provide that the market place is to be acquired pursuant to
the provisions of this Act .
(2 ) A city of the third class may proceed under this act
jointly with the county in which the city is located , in which
case the fiscal court of the county , before the bonds are is-
sued , shall adopt a parallel or companion resolution to the
ordinance of the city referred to in subsection ( 1 ) of this sec-
tion . In case of such joint action , all provisions of this Act
referring to the city or to the legislative body of the city shall
be deemed to include the county or the fiscal court of the
county , as the case may be .
§ 3. ( 1 ) The bonds may be issued to bear interest at
a rate not to exceed six percent per annum , payable either
annually or semiannually , and shall be executed in such man-
ner and be payable at such times not exceeding twenty -five
years from date thereof and at such place or places as the
city legislative body determines .
(2 ) The bonds may provide that they or any of them
may be called for redemption prior to maturity , on interest
payment dates not earlier than one year from the date , of is-
suance of the bonds , at a price not exceeding 103 and accrued

§ 4. Bonds issued pursuant to the this Act shall be
negotiable and shall not be subject to taxation . If any officer
whose signature or countersignature appears on the bonds or
coupons ceases to be such officer before delivery of the bonds ,
his signature or countersignature shall nevertheless be valid.
and sufficient for all purposes the same as if he had remained

may provide that payment shall be made in such bonds . In
no event shall any bonds be negotiated on a basis to yield

of section 3 of this Act . The bonds shall be payable solely
from the revenue derived from the market place , and shall
not constitute an indebtedness of the city within the meaning
of the Constitution . It shall be plainly stated on the face of
each bond that it has been issued under the provisions of this
Act and that it does not constitute an indebtedness of the city
within the meaning of the Constitution .
§ 5. All money received from the bonds shall be applied
solely for the acquisition of the market place , and the neces-
sary expense of preparing , printing and selling said bonds , or
to advance the payment of the interest on the bonds during
the first three years following the date of the bonds .
§ 6. If there is any default in the payment of principal
or interest of any bond , any court having jurisdiction of the
action may appoint a receiver to administer the market place
on behalf of the city , with power to charge and collect rates ,
charges and fees sufficient to provide for the payment of any
bonds or obligations outstanding against the market place ,
and for the payment of operating expenses , and to apply the
income and revenue in conformity with this Act and the ordi-
nance referred to in Section 2 of this Act .
§ 7. At or before the issuance of bonds the city legisla-
tive body shall , by ordinance , set aside and pledge the income
and revenue of the market place into a separate and special
fund to be used and applied in payment of the cost thereof
and in the maintenance , operation and depreciation thereof .
The ordinance shall definitely fix and determine the amount
of revenue necessary to be set apart and applied to the pay-

CHAPTER 25
(H. B. 94)
AN ACT relating to the expenses of elections .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 118.450 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) The printing and delivery of ballots and cards of
instruction to voters shall, in city elections , be paid for by the
city. In all other elections , the printing of the ballots and
cards of instruction for the voters in each county , and the
delivery of them to the several voting precincts , shall be paid
for by the county .
( 2 ) The cost of all elections held in any county shall be
allowed by the fiscal court and paid by the county treasurer
except as otherwise provided by law .
(3) When the cost of any election has been allowed by
the fiscal court and paid by the county treasurer , as provided
in this section or in any other section requiring the county to
pay the cost of an election , the county treasurer shall certify
the amount of such expenses , indicating each item of expense
separately , together with a statement of the number of pre-
cincts in the county , the date and kind of election to the De-
partment of Finance . Upon receipt of such certification and
upon being satisfied as to the correctness thereof, the Depart-
ment of Finance shall issue its warrant upon the State Treas-
urer in favor of the county treasurer for one-half of the

county . All sums paid under this section shall be in addition
to any sum paid by the state for ballot paper in elections .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the expenses of elections.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 118.450 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning election expenses. It clarifies that cities pay for city election printing and delivery of ballots and instruction cards, while counties pay for all other elections. The cost of all elections in a county is to be allowed by the fiscal court and paid by the county treasurer. The state Department of Finance will then reimburse the county treasurer for one-half of the election expenses, up to a maximum of twenty dollars per precinct.
classification.reasoning: The statute clearly outlines the financial responsibilities for conducting elections at the city and county levels, and the reimbursement process from the state. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to bonds of public officers and employees.
classification.summary: This act establishes requirements for bonding officers and employees within state government departments and commissions who handle money, licenses, or property. It specifies that the head of constitutional and statutory administrative departments, the Auditor of Public Accounts, the Clerk of the Court of Appeals, and the Director of the Division of Game and Fish must require bonds from their personnel involved in financial transactions. It also mandates that boards and commissions exercising executive or administrative functions, or receiving state appropriations, must require bonds from their chief fiscal agents and any personnel handling money, licenses, or property. The amounts of these bonds are to be fixed by the Commissioner of Finance.

classification.title: KRS 62.180 and KRS 62.190
classification.summary: This statute establishes new sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) concerning official bonds. KRS 62.180 defines the covenant of an official bond as a promise to faithfully discharge duties and account for all money and property. KRS 62.190 allows for the procurement of blanket or position bonds for officers and employees, with exceptions for field agents and accountants of the Department of Revenue who must execute individual bonds. It also grants the Commissioner of Finance the authority to require such bonds when deemed in the state's best interest.

classification.title: Amendments to Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning Public Printing, Registration and Purgation, and State Fair Board Disbursements
classification.summary: This statute amends several sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. Section 35 amends KRS 57.010 regarding the qualifications of the Superintendent of Public Printing, requiring extensive experience in the printing trade and prohibiting current ownership or stock in a printing business. Section 36 amends KRS 117.340 to establish a State Board of Registration and Purgation composed of the same members as the State Board of Election Commissioners, requiring an oath of office. Section 37 repeals KRS 262.080. Section 38 amends KRS 247.170 concerning the disbursement of funds for premiums to the State Fair Board, requiring checks to be countersigned by a designated agent of the Commissioner of Finance who will maintain an office on the fair grounds during the annual fair.

tax levy provided by this Act . In addition to or in lieu of this
special tax, any board of education may pay into this special
fund at the close of any fiscal year the proceeds from the sale
of land or property no longer needed for school purposes and
all or any balances remaining in the general fund over and
above the amount necessary for discharging obligations for
the fiscal year in full .
The special fund provided for herein shall be kept in a
separate account designated as " School Building Fund . " The
fund shall be kept in the depository selected by the board of
education , or invested in bonds of the United States , of this
state , or county or municipality in this state , provided how-
ever , that such investments shall be approved by the State
Board of Education .
All expenditures from such fund shall be made solely for
the purposes enumerated herein and shall be made in accord-
ance with the school laws of the state at such times as the
board of education determines . The board of education shall
cause to be made annually an audit of the building fund by a
certified public accountant or by an accountant approved by
the State Department of Education .
Section 2. All existing subdistrict school tax levies , ex-
cept those required to retire voted bonds , are hereby abolished .
Section 3. Sections 160.150 , 160.480 , 160.490 , and 162.390
to 162.435 , inclusive , of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , are
repealed .

of these employees of the same employer in the same or most
similar type of employment .
(2 ) If the employe , at the time of the injury , is regularly
employed in a higher grade of work or occupation than for-
merly during the year, and with larger regular wages , only
such higher grade of work or occupation , if it is not seasonal ,
shall be taken into consideration in computing his average
weekly wages .

deducting the number of weekly payments made to the de-
cedent from the number of weeks allowed in the original
award .

and his dependents shall not exceed the amount now payable
for death at the average weekly wage of the decedent at the
time of his injury , and the weekly payments to the dependents
shall not exceed the sum of fifteen dollars a week .

CHAPTER 38 99
PART FOUR
1.
All receipts , fees and/or revenue , regardless of whether
heretofore specified in this Act , of every department , board ,
commission , institution , agency , division , or subdivision of the
State Government shall be covered into the State Treasury
promptly , as provided by statute , through the Department of
Finance and placed to the credit of the General Expenditure
Fund, except those revenues which are specifically allocated
to the State Road Fund or the Revolving Fund which shall be
credited to their respective funds as provided for in this Act .
The fees , receipts , and/or revenue of every officer , depart-
ment , division , subdivision , commission , board , institution ,
or agency of the State Government shall be covered into the
State Treasury promptly , as provided by statute , and placed
to the credit of the General Expenditure Fund and shall be
used to defray the expenses of the State Government and the
appropriations made from the General Expenditure Fund in
this Act , unless otherwise specifically appropriated to one of
the professional boards , commissions , institutions , agencies ,
or subdivision of the State Government for Revolving Fund
purposes ; such agencies shall be only those that are listed in
this Act as having been approved for Revolving Fund Ac-
counts .
2.
The appropriations herein made for the ordinary recur-
ring expenses and extraordinary expense and capital outlay
for any officer , department , board , commission , institution ,
and subdivision of the State Government of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky shall not be available for expenditure
until allocated as now provided by law , and such appropria-
tions as specifically made in this Act are hereby declared to
be maximum and conditional appropriations except when the
Governor determines an emergency exists in any department ,
board, commission , institution , or subdivision of the State
Government requiring an additional allotment of funds to be
made from the General Emergency Fund hereby placed at his
disposal and made a specific appropriation to be used by him
to meet existing emergencies so determined by him . This
appropriation is designated under section 4, " EXECUTIVE
DEPARTMENT " , Item (d ) , Part One of this Act .

As the needs of each officer , department , board , commis-

authorized , empowered and directed by the Legislature to
prevent expenditures in excess of the amounts made available
by this Act for the purpose of meeting appropriations , by
equitably reducing , in proportion to the amounts of the
respective appropriations , and without discrimination , the
appropriations herein made to any officer , department , board ,

ment ; provided , that the power hereby vested shall not per-
mit any reduction of the appropriation of any officer , de
partment, board, commission , institution or subdivision of
the State Government that will actually impair the necessary
constitutional function of government .
Any unexpected or unencumbered balance appropriated
and designated for capital outlay remaining at the end of
the fiscal year of 1946-1947 shall not lapse to the General Ex-
penditure Fund , but may be encumbered and expended in the
fiscal year of 1947-1948 .

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1946
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for the Commissioner of Finance to manage state government expenditures and revenues for the fiscal years 1946-1947 and 1947-1948. It grants the Commissioner the authority to reduce appropriations equitably if revenues fall short of anticipated amounts, with the caveat that such reductions cannot impair the constitutional functions of government. It also specifies that unencumbered capital outlay funds from 1946-1947 can be carried over to 1947-1948. Additionally, it details how excess revenues above $39 million (up to $2.225 million) for 1946-1947 are to be appropriated for ordinary recurring expenses in 1947-1948.

It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. The provisions for equitable reduction of appropriations and the carry-over of capital outlay funds are purely administrative and financial in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

All continuing appropriations in existence at the time.

graph (k ) of Item 26 of Part One of Chapter 1 of the Acts of

Passed March 11 , enrolled March 18 , approved in part and vetoed in part ,
March 28 , 1946 .
HOUSE BILL NO. 124
(Senate Substitute )
VETO MESSAGE ON FOUR DESIGNATED ITEMS AND APPROVAL OF
ALL THE REMAINING PARTS AND ITEMS OF THE BILL .
TO THE GENERAL ASEMBLY :
I have approved House Bill No. 124 ( Senate Substitute ) except four
items as follows :
(1) On pages 11 , under Department of Conservation , Division of
State Parks , for capital outlay 1947-48 , $ 450,000.00
( 2 ) On page 25 , under State Teachers ' Colleges , Eastern State
Teachers ' College , for capital outlay , 1947-48 , $ 135,000.00 .
(3) On page 25 , under State Teachers ' Colleges , Murray State
Teachers College , for capital outlay , 1947-48 , $ 225,000.00 .
(4 ) On page 25 , under State Teachers ' Colleges , Western State
Teachers College , for capital outlay , 1947-48 , $ 200,000.00 .
The items disapproved will free over a million dollars for a margin
of safety in financing the second year of the biennium . The capital outlays
for the first year will be as much as can be reasonably expended by the
various agencies . The work contracted can be carried three months be-
yond July 1, 1947 , and if conditions are favorable at the time , and further
capital outlay is then desirable , the Legislature will meet in January , 1948 ,
and can provide for it in the light of conditions confronting them . It is
not deemed wise to freeze this large amount of money when the appro-
priations are in excess of the official estimates of income.
For these reasons I have vetoed the items mentioned above , and have
approved the remaining parts of House Bill No. 124 .
SIMEON WILLIS
Governor
Frankfort , Kentucky
March 28 , 1946

CHAPTER 38 103
STATEMENT BY THE GOVERNOR
RE : APPROPRIATIONS
HOUSE BILL 124
(Senate Substitute )
TO THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY AND WHOM IT MAY CONCERN :
When it became apparent that my recommendations for the Budget
would be exceeded , it was my obvious obligation to aid in obtaining the
most reasonable approximation to a sound budget that was possible . The
clear choice was to accept the Senate Substitute , and I did so . The total
appropriations made by Senate Substitute , as shown by a statement of the
Finance Deparment , was $87,875,812.00 for the fiscal years 1946-1947 and
1947-1948 . This is made up of $ 44,108,306.00 for the first fiscal year and
$ 43,767,506.00 for the second fiscal year . It includes $ 5,286,000.00 for capital
outlay during the first fiscal year and $ 4,510,000.00 for the second fiscal
year .
These appropriations exceed the official estimates of revenue as
shown by the Budget Report . Moreover , the Legislature made additional
appropriations in very substantial sums .
One Senate Bill No. 237 appropriates $ 20,000.00 a year for the State
Educational Agency for Surplus Property . I can control the life of this
agency , and as soon as its function is finished , the remaining funds can
be saved . Certainly it can complete its work within a year . It is important
that the schools have this service for a reasonable time .
The Military Department is being pressed to provide armories for the
care of property being given to the State by the War Department , and to
facilitate the reorganization of the National Guard . The need for that
money is immediate , and I have approved the Military bills .
The Veterans Division was abolished , but it is necessary that it carry
on during the current fiscal year . It is running short of funds , and House
Bill No. 528 is a deficiency appropriation to maintain the present level of
service until the new agency takes over on July 1st . That bill appro-
priates $ 15,000.00 for the Division , but General May will allot only the
amount necessary to see that the service to the Disabled Veterans is main-
tained while the Division remains in charge of that service .
For these reasons , and with this understanding , I have approved that
bill . The many additional appropriations have made it necessary to veto
some items in the Budget Bill in order to free funds , as a margin of safety .
The capital outlays for the parks , and the teachers colleges for the
second year of the biennium can be cut out without any material risk ,
and thereby free considerable money for financing the second year of the
next biennium .
The first fiscal year will end June 30 , 1947 , and obligations can be
met for three months thereafter . That will leave but a short period of
three months until the 1948 General Assembly will be in session , and no
harm can come from that short delay . It will provide some cushion for
the operation of the Budget . Moreover , it will leave as much capital outlay

regulating the assessment value thereof and the evidence of such

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
(1 ) That to the extent that the present right to receive

the sole basis of the assessment value shall be the fair cash
value estimated at the price that the particular right to receive
income in question would bring at a fair voluntary sale . The
value of such a right determined on the assumption that the
recipient of such income will live to some future date deter-
mined by reference to a Mortality Table shall not be substi-
tuted for the fair cash value estimated at the price that the

evidence of , or determine the assessment value by the use of

right to receive income will be enjoyed .

from any source for a life or lives or other indeterminate

life insurance policy , payable by reason of the death of the in-
sured , may be held to be subject to assessment for ad valorem
taxes , the owner or person enjoying such right on July 1st of

CHAPTER 40
(H. B. 130 )
AN ACT relating to deadly weapons .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 435.230 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
(1 ) Any person , not expressly authorized by law , who
carries concealed a deadly weapon , other than an ordinary
pocket knife , on or about his person , or any person who sells a
deadly weapon , other than an ordinary pocket knife , to a
minor , shall be confined in the penitentiary for not less than
two nor more than five years .
( 2 ) Sheriffs , constables , marshals , policemen and other
ministerial officers , when necessary for their protection in the
discharge of their official duties ; United States mail carriers ,
when actually engaged in their duties ; and agents and mes-
sengers of express companies , when necessary for their pro-
tection in the discharge of their official duties , may carry
concealed deadly weapons on or about their persons .
Passed March 1, enrolled March 4, 1946 , not approved .

(H. B. 140 )
AN ACT establishing a public park on the Old State Capitol Building
grounds in Frankfort, Kentucky , and providing for the operation ,
control , supervision and management thereof .
WHEREAS , the State Historical Society is housed and maintained in one
of the Old State Capitol buildings ; and
WHEREAS , a large number of people visit the State Historical exhibits
in said building ; and
WHEREAS , the Commonwealth of Kentucky owns and maintains real
estate adjacent to the Old State Capitol buildings ; and
WHEREAS , there are beautiful shade trees upon said grounds , and there
is adequate space upon said grounds not now utilized for other pur-
poses ; now , therefore
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That all of the Old State Capitol grounds , ex-
cept that portion thereof upon which the Old State Capitol
buildings are located and except parking space now laid off
for use in connection with said buildings , shall be and hereby
are dedicated as a public park .
Section 2. The Department of Conservation , through its
Division of State Parks , shall have control and supervision of
the park hereby established . Said division of parks shall de-
velop said park and provide appropriate benches for seating ,
appropriate play-grounds , and appropriate rest rooms and
toilet facilities thereon , and shall be charged with the duty of
supervising , maintaining and operating said park , and the
cost thereof shall be paid out of the appropriation to the De-
partment of Conservation , Division of State Parks .
No charges of any kind or character shall be made for
admission to or use of said public park .
Section 3. The public park hereby established shall be
named and known as the " OLD STATE CAPITOL PARK .”
Passed March 5 , enrolled March 6 , approved March 14 , 1946 .

Section 2 : Section 81.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
81.030 . Transfer of city to another class .
( 1 ) The General Assembly may assign any city to the
class to which the population of the city entitles it to belong ,
and in so doing may assign the city to the next higher or the
next lower class , or to any other class , in one act . For this pur-
pose the population shall be determined by a census taken by
the city , or , if no such census has been taken and there is no
other satisfactory information as to population , then by the
last preceding Federal census .
( 2 ) Any city may , by ordinance , have a census taken to
determine whether the population of the city entitles it to be
assigned to another class . The General Assembly may consider
the census in determining whether the city shall be assigned
to another class .
(3) When the General Assembly has assigned a city to
another class , the city shall thereafter be governed by and
under the general laws relating to the class to which it has been
assigned , but the transfer from one class to another shall not
affect any ordinance therefore enacted by the city , except that
any ordinance in conflict with the general laws relating to
cities of the class to which the city has been assigned shall be
repealed to the extent it so conflicts . The powers , rights , duties
or obligations of the city , or of any officer , employee , debtor
or creditor of the city , shall not be affected by the transfer .

lished for the city .

vided for in KRS 81.050 , shows it to belong shall apply to the
city .
(2 ) If the city has not been assigned to another class
before the first regular election held after its establishment at
which officers for cities may be elected , an election shall be
held as provided in the law relating to cities of the class to
which the population of the city, as determined by the esti-
mate of the number of inhabitants provided for in KRS
81.050 , shows it to belong .
Section 7 : The present uncertainty with respect to
whether a city may be transferred to any class other than
the next higher or the next lower class , and the rapid growth
in recent years of certain cities , and certain areas proposed
to be established as cities , constitute an emergency , and this
Act shall become a law and be effective immediately upon its
passage and approval by the Governor .
Passed February 27 , enrolled March 1, approved March 13 , 1946 .

112 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 43
(H. B. 150 )
AN ACT relating to fees and commissions allowed county court clerks .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
That the county court clerk, in each county , shall be al-
lowed five per cent commission on the amounts collected for
state taxes on legal processes and instruments provided for
under Section 142.010 , Kentucky Revised Statutes , 1944
( Second Edition ) , said five per cent commission to be re-
tained by the county clerk on said sums reported to the De-
partment of Finance and paid by the county clerk into the
state treasury .
Passed March 6 , enrolled March 7 , approved March 14 , 1946 .
!
CHAPTER 44
(H. B. 151 )
AN ACT to repeal , amend and reenact Section 117.140 , Kentucky Re-
vised Statutes, 1944 ( Second Edition ) , relating to fees allowed
county clerk .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
1. Section 117.140 , Kentucky Revised Statutes , 1944
( Second Edition ) , be repealed , amended and reenacted , so
that when reenacted said section shall read , as follows :
(1 ) The county clerk is entitled to collect :
( a) For original registration , two cents for each person
registered .
(b ) For copying from the original registry into the
copy book , or copying from the original for the use of any
person, two cents per name , including data and remarks .
( c ) For making copies of any registration books lost ,
mutilated or destroyed , two cents per name , including data.
and remarks .
(d ) For preparing the lists of registered voters for use
of election officers as required by KRS 117.110 , one cent for
each name listed on the original copy only .
( 2) The fees shall be paid by the county , except where
the copying is done for an individual at his request .
Passed March 13 , enrolled March 15 , approved March 22 , 1946 .

CHAPTER 46 113
CHAPTER 45
(H. B. 158 )
AN ACT relating to assistant county attorneys in certain counties.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. A new section of the statutes is created , to read as
follows :
(1 ) The county attorney in counties containing a city
of the second , third or fourth class may appoint an assistant
county attorney for the duration of his term , subject to re-
moval at any time by the county attorney .
( 2 ) The assistant county attorney shall receive an an-
nual salary to be fixed by the fiscal court at not less than
$1200.00 nor more than $ 1800.00 and paid out of the fees
received by the County Attorney . He shall possess the quali-
fications required of a county attorney and shall have the
same powers and perform the same duties as the county at-
torney , subject to the direction and control of the county at-
torney . In the absence of the county attorney , the assistant
shall act as county attorney , without additional compensation .
§ 2. Subsection ( 1 ) of Section 69.275 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(1 ) The office of assistant county attorney in counties.
having a population of less than 150,000 and not containing
a city of the second class , is hereby created .
Passed March 6 , enrolled March 7 , approved March 14 , 1946 .
CHAPTER 46
(H. B. 159 )
AN ACT relating to Workmen's Compensation .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
The Department of Welfare may elect coverage under the
Workmen's Compensation Law for employes in all institu-
tions under its control and supervision .
Passed March 20 , enrolled March 21 , approved March 23 , 1946 .

114 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
AN ACT relating to Fences .
CHAPTER 47
(H. B. 160 )
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 256.040 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) When a division fence is desirable , or is made neces-
sary by the division of improved or inclosed lands , or when
no fence exists between the improved or inclosed lands of
adjoining owners , or improved lands where the right of way
is owned by one person , either landowner may , after he has
built a lawful fence upon his portion of the line , require the
other to erect a lawful fence out of planks , rails , or wire upon
his portion of the line . No barbed wire shall be used , without
the consent of owners of land on both sides of the fence . If
the other fails to erect the fence within thirty days after
notice is given in writing , addressed and mailed to the post-
office nearest the land concerned , the person desiring the fence
shall post a reasonable number of written signs or notices
along the course on which he proposes to erect the fence , giv-
ing a description of the type and length of the fence proposed
to be erected . If the other landowner still fails to erect the
fence , the person desiring the fence may erect the fence after
a period of thirty days has elapsed from the posting of writ-
ten signs describing the proposed fence , and one -half the cost
of erection shall constitute a lien , superior to all others , in
favor of the person desiring the fence and upon the land of
the person failing to erect the fence and shall be enforced as
other liens , when such lien has been duly recorded in the
County Court Clerk's office of the County in which the land,
or a major part thereof, is situated .
( 2 ) Subsection ( 1 ) shall not apply where the person
who erects the fence has received compensation for fencing
his entire line , or where written agreements concerning the
fencing have been entered into .
Passed March 6 , enrolled March 7 , 1946 , not approved .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1946, p. 131

It also specifies that barbed wire cannot be used without mutual consent and provides exceptions for existing agreements or prior compensation for fencing.

CHAPTER 48 115
CHAPTER 48
AN ACT relating to Aviation .
(H. B. 165 )
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. 183.010 of the 1944 edition of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is repealed , amended , and reenacted so that
when amended and reenacted it will read as follows :
( 1 ) As used in chapter 183 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes , unless the context otherwise requires :
( a) "Aircraft " means any contrivance now known ,
or hereafter invented , used or designated for navigation of or
flight in the air .
(b) " Public aircraft " means an aircraft used exclu-
sively in the service of any government or of any political
subdivision thereof , including the government of any state ,
territory , or possession of the United States , or the District
of Columbia , but not including any government - owned aircraft
engaged in carrying persons or property for commercial
purposes .
( c) "Civil aircraft " means any aircraft other than a
public aircraft .
(d) " Airport " means any area , of land or water, which
is designed for the landing and take -off of aircraft , whether
or not facilities are provided for the shelter , servicing , or re-
pair of aircraft , or for receiving or discharging passengers or
cargo , and all appurtenant areas used or suitable for airport
building or other airport facilities , and all appurtenant rights
of way, whether heretofore or hereinafter established except
the word " airport " as the word is used in KRS 183.430 to
183.510 , shall be synonymous with " landing field , " or " avia-
tion field , " or " landing strip , " and shall indicate and mean
any place constructed for the landing or dispatch of machines
or aircrafts in the navigation of the air , and which has been
approved and designated for such purposes by the Kentucky
Aeronautics Commission .
( e) " Airport board " or " board " (except in KRS
183.140 to 183.220 ) means a board appointed as provided in
KRS 183.390 , but this board may be known by whatever title
the city or county , or city and county , may designate .

Section 4. (1 ) The Kentucky Aeronautics Commission
may designated , design , and establish , expand , or modify a

State . It may chart such airways system and arrange for

federal airways system . It may include all types of air naviga-
tion facilities , whether publicly or privately owned , provided
that such facilities conform to federal safety standards .

deems necessary , prepare and publish a State Airport Plan
for the purpose of coordinating and effecting a state and
national system of airports . The Commission shall , in the
preparation of said plan , take into consideration the local
needs for an airport , the state and national system of air-

OF APPROVAL AND LICENSES : In determining whether
it shall issue a certificate of approval or license for the use or
operation of any proposed airport , the Commission shall take
into consideration its proposed location , size and layout , the
relationship of the proposed airport to a comprehensive plan
for state -wide and nation-wide development , whether there
are safe areas avilable for expansion purposes , whether the
adjoining area is free from obstructions based on a proper
glide ratio , the nature of the terrain , the nature of the uses to
which the proposed airport will be put , and the possibilities
for future development .
(3 ) REVOCATION OF CERTIFICATE OF AP-
PROVAL AND LICENSES -The Commission is empowered
to temporarily or permanently revoke any certificate of ap-
proval or license issued by it when it shall determine that an
airport or other navigation facility is not being maintained
or used in accordance with the provisions of this act and the
rules and regulations lawfully promulgated pursuant thereto .
Section 7. AIRPORT PROTECTION PRIVILEGES
-Where necessary , in order to provide unobstructed air
space for the landing and taking off of aircraft utilizing air-
ports acquired or operated under the provisions of this act ,
the Kentucky Aeronautics Commission is hereby granted
authority to acquire , in the same manner as is provided for
the acquisition of property for airport purposes , easements
through or other interests in air space over land or water ,
interests in airport hazards outside the boundaries of the air-
ports and such other airport protection privileges as are
necessary to insure safe approaches to the landing areas of
said airports , and the safe and efficient operation thereof .
The Kentucky Aeronautics Commission is also hereby author-
ized to acquire , in the same manner , the right or easement , for
a term of years or perpetually , to place or maintain suitable
marks for the daytime marking and suitable lights for the
nighttime marking of airport hazards , including the right of
ingress and egress to or from such airport hazards for the
purpose of maintaining and repairing such lights and marks.
This authority shall not be so construed as to limit the right ,
power , or authority of the state or any municipality to zone
property adjacent to any airport pursuant to any law of this
state .

2. AIRPORT HAZARDS NOT IN PUBLIC INTER-
EST . It is hereby found and declared that an airport hazard
endangers the lives and property of users of the airport and
of occupants of land in this vicinity , and also , if of the ob-
struction type , in effect reduces the size of the area available
for landing , taking -off and maneuvering of aircraft , thus
tending to destroy or impair the utility of the airport and the
public investment therein , and is therefore not in the interest
of the public health , public safety or general welfare .
3. ADOPTION OF AIRPORT ZONING REGULA-
TIONS . ( 1 ) Every political subdivision may adopt , adminis-
ter, and enforce , under the police power and in the manner
and upon the conditions hereinafter prescribed , airport zon-
ing regulations for any airport within its jurisdiction , which
regulations shall divide the area surrounding the airport into
zones , and , within such zones , specify the land uses permitted ,
and regulate and restrict the height to which structures and
trees may be erected or allowed to grow . In adopting or re-
vising any such zoning regulations, the political subdivision
shall consider , among other things , the character of the flying
operations expected to be conducted at the airport , the nature
of the terrain , the height of existing structures and trees
above the level of the airport , the possibility of lowering or
removing existing obstructions , and the views of the agency of
the Federal Government charged with the fostering of civil
aeronautics , as to the aerial approaches necessary to safe fly-
ing operations at the airport .
( 2 ) In the event that a political subdivision has adopted ,
or hereafter adopts , a general zoning ordinance regulating ,
among other things , the height of buildings , and airport zon-
