128 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
N
ready exists , it may be appointed as the Board of Appeals .
Otherwise , the Board of Appeals shall consist of five mem-
hers, each to be appointed for a term of three years and to
be removable for cause by the appointing authority upon
written charges and after public hearing .
The Board shall adopt rules in accordance with the pro-
visions of any ordinance adopted under this Act . Meetings
of the Board shall be held at the call of the chairman and at
such other times as the Board may determine . The chairman ,
or in his absence the acting chairman , may administer oaths
and compel the attendance of witnesses . All meetings of the
Board shall be public . The Board shall keep minutes of its
proceedings , showing the vote of each member upon each
question , or , if absent or failing to vote , indicating such fact ,
and shall keep records of its examinations and other official
actions , all of which shall immediately be filed in the office
of the Board and shall be a public record .
Appeals to the Board may be taken by any person ag-
grieved , or by any officer , department , board, or bureau of
the political subdivision affected , by any decision of the ad-
ministrative agency . An appeal must be taken within a reason-
able time , as provided by the rules of the Board , by filing with
the agency from which the appeal is taken and with the Board ,
a notice of appeal specifying the grounds thereof . The agency
from which the appeal is taken shall forthwith transmit to the
Board all the papers constituting the record upon which the
action appealed from was taken .
An appeal shall stay all proceedings in furtherance of the
action appealed from , unless the agency from which the ap-
peal is taken certifies to the Board , after the notice of appeal
has been filed with it , that by reason of the facts stated in the
certificate , a stay , would , in its opinion , cause imminent peril
to life or property . In such case proceedings shall not be
stayed otherwise than by a restraining order which may be
granted by the Board or by a court of record on application
of notice to the agency from which the appeal is taken and on
due cause shown .
The Board shall fix reasonable time for the hearing of
the appeal , give public notice and due notice to the parties in
interest, and decide the same within a reasonable time . Upon
the hearing any party may appear in person or by agent or by
attorney .

It redefines 'Civil Service' and 'Employe', and includes provisions for administrative or directorial positions to continue without competitive examination under certain age and service conditions. It also allows cities of the second or third class to elect to operate under specific KRS sections.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with civil service employment regulations for cities of the second and third classes in Kentucky. It amends existing statutes related to definitions of civil service and employees, and outlines conditions for certain positions to be exempt from competitive examinations. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend Sections 90.330 and 90.350 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes relating to civil service commissions.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning civil service commissions in cities of the second and third classes. It redefines eligibility requirements for civil service examinations, including age (21-45), being a law-abiding citizen of sobriety and integrity, a registered voter, and the ability to read, write, and understand English. It also outlines the process for civil service appointments, emphasizing selection from the top three certified applicants and considering seniority in case of vacancies.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the establishment and operation of civil service commissions in Kentucky cities. It details eligibility criteria for applicants and the appointment process. While it sets requirements such as age, citizenship, voter registration, and language proficiency, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The requirements are general and apply to all potential applicants regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it refers to amendments to the 'Kentucky Revised Statutes'.

classification.title: An Act to amend Sections 90.360, 90.370 and 90.380 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, relating to civil service in cities of the second and third classes.
classification.summary: This act amends sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning civil service in cities of the second and third classes. It redefines the grounds for dismissal, suspension, or reduction in pay for employees in the classified service, limiting these actions to inefficiency, misconduct, insubordination, or violation of law involving moral turpitude. It also clarifies appeal processes for civil service decisions and grants the city legislative body the authority to fix employee numbers, classifications, and salaries by ordinance.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided deals with the regulations and procedures for civil service employees in cities of the second and third classes in Kentucky. It outlines the conditions under which employees can be dismissed, suspended, or have their pay reduced, and details the appeal process. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on employment regulations and administrative procedures. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification has been fixed by ordinance , no employe shall be

"And provided , further , that : Whenever in the exercise
of a reasonable discretion , it shall be the judgment of the
legislative body of the city that economic necessity requires
it , or that there is no longer a need for a particular office or
position to exist, the Board of Commissioners may abolish
said office or position and any officer or employe occupying
said office or position may be laid off or suspended until and
if such office or position is re -created or re -established . The
abolition of any office or position must not be a subterfuge to
effect another purpose , but must be actual and bona fide and
must not amount to the mere alteration , modification or
abolition of title only .
" Should any officer or employe conceive that he has
been aggrieved by the abolition or the proposed abolition of
said office or position , he may, at any time within ninety
days , file a petition in equity in the Circuit Court of the county
wherein the city is located , stating his reason why the position
or office has not been fairly abolished , or why it should not be
abolished , and upon issue joined thereon by the city , the bur-
den shall be on the latter to establish the necessity for and the
good faith of the city in abolishing said office or position . The
right to abolish and the right to have the office or position
re -created shall be determined as other equitable actions are
determined .
" If said office or position be re -created or re - established
within five years , then any person who was deprived of his
office or position shall be restored to the office or position he
formerly held or occupied in the order of his seniority if he
shall elect to do so , and it shall further be the duty of the city
to advise said officer or employee at his last known address
and to advertise at least once in the official newspaper or , if
there be none , in any newspaper of general circulation in the
city, that said office or position has been re -created or re-
established . "
Passed March 13 , enrolled March 20 , approved March 23 , 1946 .

classification.title: An Act relating to the abolition of municipal offices and positions.
classification.summary: This act outlines the procedures and rights related to the abolition of municipal offices and positions by legislative bodies of cities. It specifies conditions under which offices can be abolished due to economic necessity or lack of need, and provides a legal recourse for employees who believe their position was unfairly abolished. It also details provisions for restoration to office if it is re-created within five years.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the abolition of municipal offices and positions, outlining the process, justifications (economic necessity, lack of need), and employee rights in such situations. It provides a legal framework for challenging these decisions and for potential reinstatement. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit racial discrimination. The language is purely procedural and economic in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. CHAPTER 51 135
CHAPTER 51
(H. B. 171 )
AN ACT to provide for the incorporation and regulation of non - profit
medical service plan corporations .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
1. As used in this Act , unless the context requires other-
wise , " Director " means the Director of Division of Insur
ance .
2. As used in this Act , unless the context requires other-
wise , " Medical service " means the general and usual services
rendered and care administered by doctors of medicine . It
shall not include hospital services , nursing services , the fur-
nishing of drugs , services rendered under the Workmen's
Compensation Act , or services rendered to a group under a
plan that is mutually acceptable to that group and to those
furnishing medical service .
3. "Medical Service plan " means a plan or arrange-
ment under which medical services are , or may be rendered to
a subscriber by a licensed physician and surgeon at the ex-
pense of a medical service plan corporation .
A medical service plan corporation shall be exempt from
all provisions of the insurance laws of this state , except as
otherwise specifically provided in this Act . No amendment
to the insurance laws shall apply to medical service plan cor-
porations , unless the amendment specifically so states .
5. A "participating physician " means a doctor of medi-
cine licensed to practice medicine or surgery in this state
under the provisions of Medical Practice Act who agrees in
writing with the medical service plan corporation to perform
the medical services specified by the corporation and at such
rates of compensation as will be determined by the Board of
Directors of the corporation and who agrees to abide by the
by -laws , rules and regulations of the corporation .
6. Every doctor of medicine licensed to practice in this
state under the provisions of the Medical Practice Act and
who is reputable and in good standing shall have the right to
become a participating physician in the medical service plan
corporation operating in the county in which he resides or
practices for general or special medical care as the case may
be under such terms and conditions as are imposed on other
participating physicians under similar circumstances . A

136 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
medical service plan corporation shall impose no restrictions
on the doctors of medicine who treat its subscribers as to
methods of diagnosis or treatment . The private physician-
patient relationship shall be maintained and the subscriber
shall at all times have free choice of any doctor of medicine
who is a participating physician in the corporation and who
agrees to accept a particular beneficiary as a patient .
7. A corporation may be organized as a medical service
plan corporation under the provisions of Chapter 273 KRS
to operate under a medical service plan and to render limited
or unlimited medical service as herein provided .
8. The articles of incorporation of every medical service
plan corporation shall be presented to both the Director and
the State Board of Health for approval . The approval shall
be endorsed in writing before the articles are filed in the of-
fice of the Secretary of State or recorded in the County
Clerk's Office .
Before the State Board of Health and the Director shall
approve said articles of incorporation , they shall determine
that the proposed corporation is for convenience and neces-
sity and in the public interest .
9. No contract for medical service shall be issued to
contract holders until it has been approved in writing by the
State Board of Health . No participating physician shall enter
into any contract until the contract has been approved in
writing by the State Board of Health .
10. No medical service plan corporation shall operate in
any county unless 51 % of the eligible licensed doctors of medi-
cine , practicing in that county , are participating physicians .
11. The rate to be charged by a medical service plan cor-
poration to the contract holders for medical service shall be
filed with the Director and shall at all times be subject to the
approval of the Director .
In order that the corporation may remain solvent , all
costs or expenses in connection with the conduct of the cor-
poration , including salaries , commission , expense allowances ,
solicitation of subscribers and overhead shall at all times be
subject to the approval of the Director . No`payment shall be
made by the corporation except for medical services or for
services actually rendered in the operation of the corpora-
tion .

CHAPTER 51 137
12. KRS 298.180 to 298.230 in regard to insurance
agents , and KRS 298.150 and 298.160 , providing penalties for
violations and for suspensions and revocation of license of
agents and prohibiting unauthorized insurance companies
from doing business in this state and that the contracts shall
be valid nevertheless , shall apply to medical service plan cor-
porations and their agents .
Every medical service plan corporation shall annually as
of March 1st file in the office of the Director a statement veri-
fied by at least two of the principal officers of the corpora-
tion , showing its financial condition on December 31st , next
preceding , which shall be in the form and contain the matters
prescribed by the Director . The Director or any person he so
designates may examine the affairs of any medical service
plan corporation and have free access to all the books , papers
and documents that relate to the business of the corporation .
They may summon and qualify a witness under oath to ex-
amine its officers , agents , or employees or other persons in
relation to the affairs and transactions and condition of the
corporation .
The cost of the examination shall be paid by the corpor-
ation as provided by law for the examination of insurance
companies .
13. No medical service plan corporation shall begin
business until it has deposited with the Custodian of Insur-
ance Securities , securities acceptable to him in the sum of
$10,000.00 to insure that it will faithfully comply with its
contracts for medical service . This security shall remain on
deposit during the corporate life of the corporation .
Reasonable additional security may be required by the
Director after the corporation has written contracts in excess
of five thousand in number .
14. The funds of the corporation shall be invested only
in securities permitted by the laws of this state for investment
of assets of life insurance companies .
15. Nothing in this Act shall be construed to modify ,
vary or repeal any law now in force relating to the practice
of medicine and surgery , excepting only as they are incon-
sistent with the provisions of this Act .

CHAPTER 52
(H. B. 172 )
AN ACT relating to Soil and Water Resources and the Department of
Conservation .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Division of Soil and Water Resources - Organiza-
tion- Functions . There is hereby created , within the Depart-
ment of Conservation , a Division of Soil and Water Re-
sources . The division shall consist of a Soil and Water Re-
sources Commission , a director , and such officers , employees
and agents as the commission may deem necessary for carry-
ing out the functions of the division . It shall be the function
of the division , through the commission and the director , to
assist soil conservation districts organized under the Ken-
tucky Soil Conservation Districts Law in carrying out the
functions , powers and duties conferred upon them by such
law .
§ 2. Soil and Water Resources Commission .
A. The Soil and Water Resources Commission shall
consist of nine members , not more than five of whom shall be
of the same political party , to be appointed by the State Com-
missioner of Conservation .
B. One such member shall be appointed from each of
the nine Congressional Districts of the State from a list of two
names submitted from each such Congressional District by
the supervisors of the soil conservation districts that have
their principal offices therein . Such members must be super-
visors of soil conservation districts located within the Con-
gressional District from which their names are submitted .
C. The term of office of each such member shall be four
years except that as to the members first appointed the Com-
missioner of Conservation shall designate three members for
terms of two years , three for three years , and three for four
years ; provided that , whenever a member of the commission

classification.title: AN ACT relating to Soil and Water Resources and the Department of Conservation.
classification.summary: This act creates a Division of Soil and Water Resources within the Department of Conservation. It establishes a Soil and Water Resources Commission, a director, and other necessary personnel. The division's function is to assist soil conservation districts in carrying out their duties. The commission will consist of nine members appointed by the State Commissioner of Conservation, with one member from each of the nine Congressional Districts, selected from lists provided by local soil conservation district supervisors. Members must be supervisors of soil conservation districts within their respective congressional districts. Terms are set at four years, with initial appointments staggered.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the organization and functions of a division within the Department of Conservation related to soil and water resources. It outlines the structure of the Soil and Water Resources Commission and the appointment process for its members, emphasizing representation from congressional districts and requiring members to be supervisors of soil conservation districts. There is no mention of race, and the subject matter does not inherently suggest any racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

3. Director of Division of Soil and Water Resources-
Appointment — Duties . Upon the organization of the commis-

agricultural college , with at least five years of practical ex-
perience in professional agricultural activities and who shall

shall serve at the will of , and receive such compensation as

upon his duties shall take the constitutional oath . The director
shall hold no other public office or employment . In addition to
any other duties assigned to him by the commission , the
director shall exercise , subject to the approval of the commis-
sion , general administrative supervision over all activities ,
employees and property of the commission .

CHAPTER 53 141
CHAPTER 53
(H. B. 178 )
AN ACT providing that the clerk of the Court in which an inquest pro-
ceeding is held for the adjudication or restoration of an idiot , feeble-
minded , epileptic or insane person or a person incompetent to
manage his estate , shall file an attested copy of the judgment of
said court in the office of the County Clerk and requiring the
County Clerk to index same and fixing penalties therefor .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
It shall be the duty of the Clerk of the Court , immediately
upon the rendering of a judgment in an inquest proceeding
adjudging a person to be an idiot , feeble -minded , epileptic or
insane , or incompetent to manage his estate or a restoration
of same, within not more than twenty -four hours thereafter ,
to file an attested copy of said judgment in the office of the
Clerk of the County Court of the County in which said judg-
ment was rendered , which copy shall be filed and not recorded
by the County Clerk but indexed in the book in which notices
of action and encumbrances are indexed . For each filing the
County Clerk shall receive a fee of fifty cents which shall be
taxed as costs of the inquest by the Clerk of the Court filing
same ; provided , however , should the person adjudged be
without any estate , or a pauper , the County Clerk shall re-
ceive , file and index said judgment without the payment of
any fee.
Unless a copy of said judgment is filed in said County
Clerk's Office , the judgment shall not constitute notice to any
subsequent bona fide purchaser for value , mortgagee , or en-
cumbrancer .
Any Clerk or Deputy Clerk of any Court charged with the
duty provided for in this act , who fails to perform said duty
shall be fined not less than twenty -five ( $25.00 ) dollars nor
more than fifty ( $ 50.00 ) dollars for each offense .
Passed February 26 , enrolled February 28 , approved March 6 , 1946 .

classification.title: AN ACT providing that the clerk of the Court in which an inquest proceeding is held for the adjudication or restoration of an idiot, feeble-minded, epileptic or insane person or a person incompetent to manage his estate, shall file an attested copy of the judgment of said court in the office of the County Clerk and requiring the County Clerk to index same and fixing penalties therefor.
classification.summary: This act mandates that the clerk of any court holding an inquest for the adjudication or restoration of a person deemed an idiot, feeble-minded, epileptic, insane, or incompetent to manage their estate must file an attested copy of the judgment with the County Clerk within 24 hours. The County Clerk is required to index this judgment, not record it, in the book used for notices of action and encumbrances. A fee of fifty cents is to be paid to the County Clerk for this service, which is to be taxed as costs of the inquest, unless the adjudged person is a pauper. If the judgment copy is not filed, it will not serve as notice to subsequent bona fide purchasers, mortgagees, or encumbrancers. Clerks failing to comply face fines.

It outlines procedural requirements for filing and indexing court judgments related to these matters. The act focuses solely on the legal and administrative aspects of handling such judgments to ensure proper notification to third parties. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 61
(H. B. 201 )
AN ACT requiring every employer now or hereafter doing business in
this State who regularly employs three or more persons in hazard-
ous occupations and who is eligible to operate under the provisions
of the Workmen's Compensation Act but who fails to do so , to file
with the Department of Industrial Relations an indemnity bond or
insurance policy which shall insure the payment of any judgment
obtained against such employer by an employee for damages result-
ing in injuries to the person or death to such employee ; defining
hazardous occupations ; providing penalties for the violation thereof ;
and providing civil remedies for the enforcement thereof .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
SECTION 1- That every employer now or hereafter do-
ing business in this Commonwealth who regularly employs
three or more persons in a hazardous occupation and who is
not excepted from the application of the provisions of the
Workmen's Compensation Act ( Chapter 342 , Kentucky Re-
vised Statutes ) but fails to elect to operate thereunder , or
having so elected withdraws such election shall file with the
Commissioner of Industrial Relations , within thirty ( 30 )
days after this Act becomes effective or such employment is
begun or such previous election is withdrawn a good and suf-
ficient security, indemnity bond or insurance policy issued
by a surety or insurance company or other insurance carrier
authorized to transact such business in this State , which shall
insure the payment of any final judgment of a court of com-
petent jurisdiction obtained against such an employer by an
employee or his personal representative for damages result-
ing from injuries to the person or death of such employee , by
accident arising out of and in the course of his employment .
The bond or policy shall contain such terms and shall be in
such penal sum or maximum amount as the Director of said
department shall deem necessary , taking into consideration
the solvency of the employer , the number of employees and
the hazard of the employment in which they are engaged for
the reasonable protection of such judgment creditors in the
collection of the amounts adjudged to be due them .
It shall be a condition of such security, indemnity bond
or insurance policy that the obligor will promptly pay such
judgments to the persons entitled thereto . Such security , in-
demnity bond and insurance policy shall be renewed annually

154 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 62
(H. B. 203 )
AN ACT relating to Fiscal Courts .
Be it enacted by the . General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 67.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
(1 ) Each county commissioner serving on a fiscal court
may be paid out of the county treasury the following compen-
sation for his services :
( a) In counties containing a city of the first or second
class , a salary of three thousand dollars per annum , payable
monthly .
(b) In counties containing a city of the third or fourth
class , a salary of not more than one thousand , eight hundred
dollars per annum , payable monthly.
(c ) In other counties , the same compensation per day
as justices of the peace receive under subsections ( 2 ) and ( 3 )
of this section .
( 2 ) Each justice of the peace serving on a fiscal court
shall be paid for his services out of the county treasury ten
dollars for each day of actual attendance of the meetings of
the fiscal court .
(3 ) County commissioners or justices of the peace shall
not receive any other compensation for their services , or any
compensation for serving on committees of the fiscal court .
Passed March 20 , enrolled March 21 , approved March 25 , 1946 .
CHAPTER 63
(H. B. 206 )
AN ACT relating to charges to grand juries .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 29.230 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
The judge of each circuit court shall charge each grand
jury impaneled in his court with reference to their duties in
regard to law enforcement .
Passed March 7 , enrolled March 8 , approved March 14 , 1946 .

CHAPTER 64
(H. B. 208)
AN ACT to establish a portion of the boundary line between Knox and
Laurel Counties .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 1. That the Park Hill and Camp Ground Road , as
it runs in a general northerly direction from the Barbourville
Highway to the Laurel County Line , is hereby found and de-
clared to be the correct boundary line beween Knox and Laurel
Counties .
Sec . 2. That the tracts of land owned by Bart Jones , the
Frazier Black Heirs , Nannie Daniels , P. R. Scalf and wife ,
and Virgil Wagners , all of which tracts of land are located on
the east side of the Park Hill and Camp Ground Road , are
hereby found and declared to be situated in Knox County .
Sec . 3. ( 1 ) The County Clerk of Laurel County is hereby
directed to certify to the County Clerk of Knox County the
titles to the various tracts of land referred to in section two of
this Act for recording in Knox County .
( 2 ) Knox and Laurel Counties shall each bear its pro-
portionate share of the costs of transfer of titles .
(3 ) The county tax commissioner of Laurel County shall
hereafter omit from his assessment lists the tracts of land
referred to in section two of this Act , and the county tax com-
missioner of Knox County shall add those tracts of land to his
next assessment lists .
Passed March 6 , enrolled March 7 , 1946 , not approved .

(H. B. 210 )
AN ACT relating to professional engineers .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 322.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes of 1944
is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) The secretary -treasurer of the board shall notify
every licensee of the date of the expiration of his license and
the amount of the fee required for its renewal for one year .

CHAPTER 67
(H. B. 223 )
AN ACT relating to higher education for Negroes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 3. Section 166.170 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The State Board of Education shall prescribe rules and
regulations governing the granting of aid under KRS 166.160 .
The tuition and fees shall be ascertained by the Superintend-
ent of Public Instruction and paid upon his requisition out of
funds appropriated for that purpose . If the funds appro-
priated for the purpose in any year are insufficient , the State
Board of Education may prorate the funds among the persons
whose applications are approved . Not more than $350.00
shall be allowed to any person under the provisions of this
section and KRS 166.160 during any one school year of nine
months .
Passed and enrolled March 21 , approved March 23 , 1946 .
CHAPTER 68
(H. B. 230)
AN ACT relating to the establishment by cities of artificial gas system .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. As used in this Act , the term " Acquire " shall mean
and include construct , acquire by purchase , by lease , devise ,
gift, or the exercise of the right of eminent domain in the
manner now or hereafter provided by law for the exercise
thereof and acquisition by any other mode .
§ 2. ( 1 ) Any city may acquire , maintain and operate
an artificial gas system , together with extensions and neces-
sary appurtenances thereto within or without the limits of the
city .

§ 3. (1 ) Before any city may acquire , maintain or op-
erate any such artificial gas system , the legislative body of
the city shall pass an ordinance declaring it desirable that this
be done , and shall prepare an estimate of the probable cost
of the system . If revenue bonds are to be issued to pay the
cost the ordinances shall so provide . An election shall be had
on the adoption of the ordinance if a number of legal voters of
the city equal to twenty -five percent of the total number of
votes cast in the city at the last regular election file a petition
within ten days after the ordinance is passed asking that the
question of approval of the ordinance be submitted to a vote
of the people . If the petition is filed the election shall be held
at the next regular election held in the city . If no petition
filed , the city may proceed immediately with the acquisition of
an artificial gas system .
( 2 ) If an election is petitioned for, the presiding officer
of the city shall certify the ordinance to the county court
clerk, who shall cause to be printed on the ballots for use in
the city the question : " Are you in favor of acquiring , main-
taining and operating an artificial gas system in accordance

insert name of city ) and the issuance of revenue bonds in the
amount of $ .. (here insert total face amount
of bonds estimated by the legislative body of the city to be
necessary to pay the cost of the system , based upon the esti-
mate of cost ) " . Opposite the question shall be printed " Yes "
and " No " , with the proper squares for stamping . The pre-
siding officer of the city shall advertise the election and its
object for at least seven days before the date of the election
in the newspaper having the largest circulation in the city ,
or by handbills posted at not less than four conspicuous places
in each voting precinct in the city and at the door of the City
Hall . All legal voters of the city may vote at the election .
( 3) If an election is held , the city shall not acquire ,
maintain or operate the system or issue revenue bonds unless
a majority of all the qualified voters voting on the question
vote in favor thereof.

mission shall be known as the " Artificial

lations and by -laws for the artificial gas system and shall ,

penses , repairs and additions thereto , and provide a sufficient

any member of the commission becomes a candidate for or is

vacate his office on the commission and another person shall

(3) Each commissioner shall execute bond with a surety
company in the penal sum of one thousand dollars , condi-

city and the premiums shall be paid by the city . An action
may be maintained upon any commissioner's bond by any
person injured by a violation of the covenants therein con-
tained .
(4) The members of the commission shall serve without
compensation .

ment , held any city, county , state or federal office , or who is

of the city . No member of the commission shall be interested
in any contract for the furnishing of supplies or services of
any kind to the system , or to the city . If at any time during
his term of office any member of the commission becomes a
candidate for or is elected or appointed to any public office ,
he shall automatically vacate his office on the commission
and another person shall be appointed .

surety company in the penal sum of one thousand dollars ,
conditioned upon the faithful performance of his official

CHAPTER 70
(H. B. 237)
AN ACT relating to Water Districts , amending Section 74.240 of The
Kentucky Revised Statutes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 74.240 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) The commission shall keep an account of the time
spent by all employes , and each item of expense incurred in
connection with any water district , and shall charge such ac-
count to the district for which the expense was incurred .
Where the time or work is upon more than one district at the
same time , it shall be apportioned between the districts . In
the event any compensation fixed by the commission for any
employe is on a salary basis , such salary shall be equitably
apportioned between the districts by the commission .
(2 ) The commission shall be required to prepare and
make available , to the consumers of the water supplied by
any water district , an annual statement of receipts and dis-
bursements ; and any floating or bonded indebtedness . This
report shall show the cost of water, material , labor , other
salaries and any other expenses incidental to the operation
and maintenance .
(3 ) All books of the commission shall be open for pub-
lic inspection during normal business hours.
Passed and enrolled March 21 , approved March 23 , 1946 .

AN ACT relating to billiard and pool rooms .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 436.320 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes of 1944
is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) Any person owning or controlling a billiard or pool
table who permits , for compensation or reward , any minor ,
without the written permission of the parent , guardian or
other person having the care , custody or control of the minor ,
to play any game on the table whether accompanied by bet-
ting or not , shall be fined one hundred dollars and shall for-
feit the right and privilege of again keeping a table .
(2 ) The permit required by subsection ( 1 ) of this sec-
tion shall be made under oath . All permits shall be filed
alphabetically by the person owning or operating the billiard
or pool table and shall be subject to inspection by any peace
officer .

It outlines the process for filing the petition, service of process, and the court's powers regarding alimony, custody, and support of children. The court can also order the plaintiff to pay for the defendant's care and burial expenses.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns divorce proceedings for individuals deemed permanently incurably insane. It details procedural requirements, court powers, and financial responsibilities related to such divorces. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the 'GENERAL ASSEMBLY' in 1946.

CHAPTER 76
(H. B. 260 )
AN ACT concerning city police and fire departments .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Any member of the police or fire department of a city
of the second or third class , who , while a member of the police
or fire department , entered the Armed Forces of the United
States , and who was honorably discharged therefrom , shall
upon his return to that police or fire department be entitled
to the same pension or benefits provided by KRS 95.560 ,
and his beneficiaries shall be entitled to the same pension
under KRS 95.550 , as if the member had remained on active
duty with that police or fire department , and his time served
in the Armed Forces shall be added to his previous service
and shall be construed for purposes of eligibility for pensions
or benefits , either for himself or his beneficiaries , as a part
and continuation of his consecutive years of service with that
police or fire department , provided this Act shall apply only
to those members who served in World War II, who apply
for reinstatement within 90 days after the date on which the
member first received or could have received an honorable
discharge , and shall not apply to those members re -enlisting
in the Armed Forces of the United States .
Passed and enrolled March 21 , approved March 23 , 1946 .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1946, p. 190

classification.title: AN ACT concerning city police and fire departments.
classification.summary: This act grants members of city police or fire departments in second or third-class cities, who served in the Armed Forces during World War II and were honorably discharged, the same pension or benefits as if they had remained on active duty. Their military service time is added to their previous service for pension eligibility. This applies to those who apply for reinstatement within 90 days of their discharge and not to those re-enlisting in the Armed Forces.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative acts. The first act (CHAPTER 76 173) deals with the dissolution and reorganization of hospital service corporations and their conversion to life insurance companies. The second act (CHAPTER 76, H. B. 260) concerns city police and fire departments and provides benefits for members who served in World War II. Neither of these acts mentions race or has any apparent discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, they are classified as not Jim Crow laws.

174 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 77
(H. B. 263 )
AN ACT authorizing circuit judges in counties containing cities of
second class having an assessed valuation of less than $ 100,000,000.00
and comprising a separate judicial district to appoint a secretary
to the master commissioner of said court , providing a salary of said
secretary and for payment thereof , and providing a schedule of
fees permitted to be charged by such secretary for making steno-
graphic reports .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
( 1 ) The circuit judge in counties containing a city of
the second class having an assessed valuation of less than
$ 100,000,000.00 and comprising a separate judicial district
may appoint a secretary to the master commissioner of the
circuit court. The secretary shall be skilled in stenographic
reporting and shall hold office for a period of four years , or
until his successor is appointed and qualified , unless sooner
removed at the pleasure of the circuit judge .
(2 ) The secretary , before entering upon the discharge
of his duties , shall take an oath before the judge of the court
for which he is appointed to faithfully discharge the duties
of his office . The appointment of the secretary and the fact
of his having qualified shall be entered upon the order book
of the court .
(3 ) The secretary shall receive a salary of not less than
$1,200.00 nor more than $ 2,400.00 per annum , to be fixed by
the fiscal court and paid in monthly installments by the
county .
( 4 ) Upon any trial or proceeding before the master
commissioner if either party request the services of the secre-
tary, or if, in the opinion of the circuit judge or master com-
missioner , the testimony should be preserved , the judge or
master commissioner shall direct the secretary to , and the
secretary shall , take full stenographic notes of the proceed-
ings . The secretary shall receive for making transcripts
thirty cents per page for original copies and twelve and one-
half cents for each duplicate thereof . The fee for making
any transcript or duplicate shall be paid by the party upon
whose motion the transcript or duplicate is made , such fees
to be taxed as part of the costs in the action .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1946, p. 191

classification.title: AN ACT authorizing circuit judges in counties containing cities of second class having an assessed valuation of less than $ 100,000,000.00 and comprising a separate judicial district to appoint a secretary to the master commissioner of said court , providing a salary of said secretary and for payment thereof , and providing a schedule of fees permitted to be charged by such secretary for making steno- graphic reports .
classification.summary: This act authorizes circuit judges in specific counties (containing a second-class city with an assessed valuation under $100,000,000 and a separate judicial district) to appoint a secretary to the master commissioner. The secretary must be skilled in stenographic reporting, hold office for four years, and take an oath. The secretary's salary, set by the fiscal court, would range from $1,200 to $2,400 annually, paid monthly by the county. The secretary would take stenographic notes of proceedings upon request or if deemed necessary by the judge or master commissioner, with fees for transcripts paid by the requesting party and taxed as costs.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and procedural aspects of a court, specifically the appointment and duties of a secretary to a master commissioner. It outlines salary, tenure, and fee structures for this role. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 78
(H. B. 266 )
AN ACT relating to Stenographers for County Attorneys in counties
containing a city of the second class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 69.330 of the Kentucky Statutes is hereby re-

69.330 ( 136c - 1 ; 136c - 2 ) Stenographer in counties con-

city of the second class , the county attorney shall appoint
a stenographer for a term of four years , subject to removal
at any time by the county attorney . The stenographer shall
receive an annual salary of not less than $600 nor more than

in equal monthly installments .
Passed March 13 , enrolled March 15 , approved March 23 , 1946 .

(H. B. 301 )

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-

§ 1. Section 247.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :

( 1 ) The State Fair Board shall be composed of nine

Governor shall be appointed from a list of six nominees sub-
mitted to the Governor by the board of directors of the Ken-
tucky Farm Bureau Federation ; one member of the second

§ 2. Section 247.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
CORPORATE POWERS OF FAIR BOARD ; RECORD
OF MEETINGS .
( 1 ) The State Fair Board shall be a body corporate
with the usual corporate powers , and shall require a complete
record to be kept of all its meetings .
(2) A quorum of the board shall consist of five members ,
and a majority of those present at any meeting of the board
may act for the board upon any matter which may legally
come before such meeting .
§ 3. Section 247.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows :
OFFICERS OF FAIR BOARD .
The State Fair Board shall elect from among such mem-
bers as the Governor shall appoint a president and a vice
president . The manager provided for in KRS 247.130 shall
be ex -officio secretary and treasurer to the board .

§ 4. Section 247.130 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows :
MANAGER , EMPLOYES AND AGENTS .
( 1 ) The State Fair Board shall appoint a manager who
shall not be one of their number . The manager shall be ap-
pointed on the basis of training , ability and experience . The
manager shall hold his position during the pleasure of the
board , but may be removed only by an affirmative vote of
six members of the board . The manager shall be responsible
for the administration of the policies set by the board , shall
devote his entire time and attention to the performance of
such duties and work as may be required of him by law or be
assigned to him by the board , and shall receive such salary ,
fixed on an annual basis , as the board may determine .
(2 ) The manager may organize such administrative
divisions as may be necessary and may designate chiefs of
such divisions who , under his control and supervision , shall
have the duties of direction of such divisions . The manager
may employ such other employes and agents as he deems
necessary for the carrying out of the policies of the board and
to conduct the affairs of the State Fair , and may fix the
duties and compensation of any employes or agents with the
approval of the board.
( 3) The board may require the manager and any em-
ploye or agent to give bond for the faithful performance of
their duties , and may pay the permium on any such bond .
(4) The manager , employes and agents provided for in
this section shall not be subject to the provisions of KRS
42.110 or 42.120 .

cise of its power of eminent domain it may proceed in the
manner provided in KRS 416.115 or in the manner provided

Passed March 7 , enrolled March 8 , 1946 , not approved .
STATEMENT OF THE GOVERNOR
HOUSE BILL NO . 301
This bill amends the law relating to the State Fair Board . It pro-
vides for three ex-officio members , as at present , and for the appoint-
ment by the Governor of six members from the State at large , with due
consideration to geographical distribution throughout the State . But
these new members can be appointed only as the terms of the present
members of the Board expire . This is so because the Act expressly
limits the power to appoint to “each two years , or as the terms of the
present six appointed members of the Board expire " .
The terms of the present six members will expire as follows :
Two terms will expire on December 12 , 1946
Two terms will expire on December 12 , 1948
Two terms will expire on December 12 , 1949
If and when vacancies occur by resignation or otherwise on the
present Board before the fixed terms expire , these vacancies will have
to be filled for the unexpired portion of the terms . No appointment can
be made under the new Act until the "terms " fixed by the old law shall
have expired . The Attorney General has made a careful and complete
analysis of the bill , and he has advised that the present Board is com-
pelled to carry on to the time fixed for the expiration of their respective
terms . This will require the Board , as now organized , to carry on through
1946 and 1947 , which will coincide with the coming into office of a new
Commissioner of Agriculture . The new plan will get into operation in
the next administration .
It is obviously the purpose of the bill for the new plan to come into
operation gradually without disrupting the present organization of the
Board , and without affecting the management of the State Fair for
the next two seasons .
I deem it unfortunate that the Commissioner of Agriculture in the
succeeding terms is made ineligible to serve as Chairman of the Board .
But that can be corrected by the next session of the General Assembly .
The term of office , set at six years , and the manner of appointment pro-
vided , indicates a desire to provide continuity of management which is
a very wise policy . The Constitution limits the power of the Legislature
in the creation of offices, to a term not exceeding four years .
(Con . Section 93 ) The Court of Appeals has held that the excess of
two years may be treated as void , and the Act remain valid for a four-
year term . (Commissioners vs. George , 104 Ky . 260 )
The policy of the law can be carried out by the Governor by reap-
pointing experienced and satisfactory members from lists furnished by

CHAPTER 81
(H. B. 307)
AN ACT relating to political subdivisions of the state and the purchase
of supplies , materials and equipment .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Whenever authorized by ordinance or resolu-
tion by its legislative authority , any political subdivision of
the State shall have power to purchase supplies , materials or
equipment from or through the United States Government
without calling for bids , notwithstanding any law or charter
provision to the contrary .
Whereas , the Federal Government is now selling many
items that are unobtainable from any other source and which
are urgently needed by municipalities .
Now , therefore , an emergency is hereby declared to exist ,
and this Act shall become effective immediately upon its
passage and approval by the Governor .
Passed and enrolled March 21 , approved March 23 , 1946 .

CHAPTER 83
(H. B. 311 )
AN ACT relating to SOIL CONSERVATION .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
1. Section 262.260 KENTUCKY REVISED STAT-
UTES , 1944 ( Second Edition ) be and the same is hereby re-
pealed , amended and reenacted so that when reenacted same
shall read as follows :
262.260 (42 :-7 ) BOARD AND CITY ALDERMEN AND /
OR COUNTY FISCAL COURT MEMBERS MAY CONFER
AND PARTICIPATE .
1. The Board may by resolution invite the legislative
body of any city and/or administrative body of any county
( Fiscal Court ) in which the district is located or nearby , to
select a representative to consult with the board of the district
on all questions of program and policy which may affect the
property , water supply, or other interests of the city or county ,
and to request by resolution that said city and/or county par-
ticipate wholly or partially in any expense attendant to the
execution of any program and/or policy setting forth in said
resolution all details of the program and/or policy together
with an estimated annual cost . Should said legislative body
approve said program and/or policy , it may expend funds
out of its respective tax levy to assist in said program and/or
policy .
Passed March 20 , enrolled March 21 , approved March 23 , 1946 .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to SOIL CONSERVATION.
classification.summary: This act repeals and reenacts Section 262.260 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, 1944 (Second Edition). It allows the Board of Soil Conservation Districts to invite city legislative bodies and county fiscal courts to select representatives to consult on program and policy matters affecting their interests. These bodies may also be requested to participate in program expenses, and if approved, can expend tax levy funds to assist.

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 84 199
Section 11. A new section of the statutes is created to be
numbered KRS 150.305 and to read as follows :
(1 ) Except as otherwise provided in this section , no per-
son shall have in his possession any wild bird , wild animal or
fish protected by this chapter , or any raw fur , except during
the open season for the particular species .
( 2 ) Any person who has legally taken any rabbit ,
squirrel , quail or grouse may have the same in his possession
in a frozen food locker , either in his home or in a commercial
locker plant , for a period of 90 -days after the close of the
season for the particular species . The name of the taker , date
of taking , and the number and species of animal or bird must
be marked on the package in which the same are stored .
Legally imported game or fish may be held in such a frozen
food locker for a period of time to conform with pure food
law , providing however , the date of storage , with the number
of the nonresident license be attached or stamped on each
package or carcass .
( 3 ) Any person who has legally taken any wild animal ,
wild bird or fish in another state and who has legally trans-
ported or shipped the same to this State in accordance with
the provisions of subsection ( 6 ) of KRS 150.180 , may have
the same in his possession , other than in a frozen food locker
as above provided , in this State for a period of not more than
10-days after the close of the open season in the state in which
the same was taken .
(4 ) Federal regulations shall govern the possession of
migratory wild birds .
( 5 ) Any person who has legally taken a fur bearing
animal, or the raw fur thereof , may have the same in his pos-
session until February 15 , following the close of the season .
( 6 ) A licensed fur buyer ( resident or nonresident ) may
buy or have in his possession raw furs until February 20 , fol-
lowing the close of the season .
(7) A licensed fur processor may buy raw furs from a
licensed fur buyer at any time during the period that a fur
buyer is authorized by this section to sell raw furs , and the
licensed fur processor is further authorized to hold raw furs
in cold storage for the purpose of manufacture or processing ,
or to buy from or sell to another licensed processor , at any
time in the year .

classification.title: KRS 150.305 - Possession of Wild Birds, Animals, Fish, and Furs
classification.summary: This statute, KRS 150.305, outlines the regulations for possessing wild birds, animals, fish, and raw furs in Kentucky. It specifies open seasons for taking these items and sets limits on how long they can be possessed after the season closes, including provisions for frozen storage and legally imported game. It also addresses possession of items taken in other states and federal regulations for migratory birds. Licensed fur buyers and processors have specific allowances for possessing and trading raw furs.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns regulations for hunting and fishing, specifically the possession of game and furs. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. The regulations apply to all individuals equally, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 12. Section 150.310 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes of 1944 is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) No person shall take a wild bird except with bow
and arrow or with a gun , which gun shall not be larger than a
10-gauge fired from the shoulder . No bird shall be taken with ,
or by means of any automatic loading or hand -operated re-
peating shotgun capable of holding more than three shells ,
the magazine of which has not been cut off or plugged with a
one-piece metal or wooden filler incapable of removal through
the loading end thereof, so as to reduce the capacity of said
gun to not more than three shells at one time in the magazine
and chamber combined .
( 2 ) No person shall hunt birds with lights or other
means used to blind birds at night .
Section 13. Subsection ( 1 ) of Section 150.330 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes of 1944 is amended to read as
follows :
( 1 ) No person shall take any wild birds except during
the open season for the particular species . The open season
for quail or bob -white shall be November 20 to January 15 .
The open season for ruffed grouse or native pheasant shall be
December 1 to December 15. There shall be no open season on
wild turkey . No person shall take quail , ruffed grouse or na-
tive pheasant except from sunrise to sunset .
Section 14. Subsection ( 2 ) of Section 150.360 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes of 1944 is amended to read as
follows :
( 2 ) A gun used in the taking of wild animals shall be no
larger than a 10 -gauge and fired from the shoulder . No wild
animals shall be taken with , or by means of , any automatic-
loading or hand -operated repeating shot -gun capable of hold-
ing more than three shells , the magazine of which has not been
cut off or plugged with a one -piece metal or wooden filler in-
capable of removal through the loading end thereof , so as to
reduce the capacity of said gun to not more than three shells
at one time in the magazine and chamber combined .
Section 15. A new section of the statutes is created to be
numbered KRS 150.365 and to read as follows :
No person shall use fire or smoke to drive game out of a
hole or a tree , or burn a field for the purpose of driving game .

CHAPTER 84 201
Section 16. Section 150.370 of Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes of 1944 is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) No person shall take any wild animal protected by
this chapter except during the open season for the particular
species . The open season for rabbits shall be November 20 to
January 15 ; the open season for mink , opossum , raccoon ,
skunk , muskrat and red fox shall be December 1 to January
31 ; for squirrel , August 15 to November 30 ; and for pond ,
bull or jumbo frogs , June 1 to May 1. Opossum and raccoon
may also be hunted and taken by dog only from November 1
to January 31. There shall be no open season for otter or
beaver .
(2 ) Subsection ( 1 ) shall not apply to persons who hunt
red fox at night with dogs for sport and not to kill ; but this
exception in favor of sportsmen shall not extend to anyone
engaged in the business of a fur dealer or a trapper or pro-
fessional hunter.
(3) The possession of any wild animals , fur bearers , or
the raw fur thereof, when the season is not open , shall be
prima facie evidence that the animal was taken out of open
season , but this presumption shall not apply to possession
of any such animal or fur by a trapper between January 31
and February 15 , or by a fur buyer between January 31 and
February 20 .
Section 17. Section 150.380 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes of 1944 is amended to read as follows :
No person shall in any one day take more than six
squirrels , eight rabbits , or fifteen pond , bull or jumbo frogs ,
nor shall any person have in his possession at any time more
than such number , except as follows :
(1 ) A person who has hunted two or more days in suc-
cession may have in his possession a total of not more than
twice the number above allowed .
(2 ) A person may have in his possession in a cold stor-
age locker , as provided in KRS 150.305 a number not exceed-
ing two days ' bag limit after the first day of the hunting
season .
( 3 ) The holder of a rabbit retail dealer's license may
have in his possession not more than 100 rabbits .
(4 ) The holder of a rabbit wholesale dealer's license
may have in his possession not more than 500 rabbits .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1946, p. 218

classification.title: Amendment to Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 150.370 and 150.380 concerning hunting seasons and bag limits
classification.summary: This statute amends existing Kentucky Revised Statutes related to hunting. It also sets daily bag limits for squirrels, rabbits, and frogs, with exceptions for possession in cold storage or for licensed dealers. Additionally, it outlines conditions for hunting red fox at night with dogs for sport.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly discriminate based on race. The regulations apply to all individuals without distinction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 18. A new section of the statutes is created to
be numbered KRS 150.411 and to read as follows :
Every licensed taxidermist shall keep a suitable record in
which he shall enter the species of each animal mounted by
him , and the name and address of the person for whom the
mounting was done . Within 10 -days after the end of each
month , he shall send to the Division of Game and Fish a re-
port showing the number and name of animals of each species
mounted by him during such month , and the name and address
of the person for which each animal was mounted . No animal
or bird shall be mounted except during open season , unless a
permit is issued by the Director , which shall be issued without
cost .
Section 19. A new section of the statutes is created , to
be numbered KRS 150.412 and to read as follows :
No commercial guide may participate in the taking of
game or fish protected by this chapter beyond the bag limit
or creel limit of the person or persons he is guiding .
Section 20. A new section of the statutes is created to be
numbered KRS 150.415 and to read as follows :
Every licensed fur buyer shall , upon purchasing any raw
fur , issue a receipt to the trapper from whom he has pur-
chased such furs , setting forth the number and kind of furs ,
the date of the purchase , the price paid for each fur , and the
license number of the trapper's license . The buyer shall make
out a duplicate receipt at the same time , and obtain the signa-
ture and address on such duplicate of the trapper from whom
the furs were purchased . Not later than March 1 of each year ,
the buyer shall send the duplicate receipts to the Division .
Failure to send the receipts in as herein directed shall bar
the licensee from obtaining a license for the following year .
Section 21. A new section of the statutes is created to be
numbered KRS 150.416 and to read as follows :
Every licensed fur processor shall , within 15 days after
January 1 and July 1 of each year , file with the Division of
Game and Fish an inventory of the furs in his possession as
of such dates . Each licensed fur processor shall also issue re-
ceipts for the total amount of furs purchased from the fur
buyers , with the license number of the fur buyer an the re-
ceipt . Duplicates of such receipts shall be sent to the Division
with the inventory report .

classification.title: An Act relating to game and fish; prescribing duties of taxidermists, commercial guides, fur buyers and fur processors.
classification.summary: This act establishes record-keeping and reporting requirements for licensed taxidermists, commercial guides, fur buyers, and fur processors in Kentucky. It mandates that taxidermists record details of animals mounted and report monthly to the Division of Game and Fish. Commercial guides are prohibited from exceeding bag or creel limits. Fur buyers must issue receipts to trappers and submit duplicate receipts annually. Fur processors must file semi-annual inventories and issue receipts for furs purchased from buyers.

CHAPTER 85
(H. B. 313 )
AN ACT to amend section 92.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , re-
lating to the fiscal year of cities other than the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 92.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
92.020 . FISCAL YEAR OF CITIES OTHER THAN
THE FIRST CLASS . The fiscal year of cities other than the
first class shall begin on the following dates :
( 1 ) In cities of the second class , January 1st ; provided ,
however , the city legislative body may designate June 1st as
the beginning date .
(2) In cities of the third class , June 1st .
( 3 ) In cities of the fourth , fifth and sixth classes , Jan-
uary 1st.
Section 2. Whereas the classification of at least one of
the cities of the second class has only recently been changed
from that of a city of the third class and the conduct of its
fiscal affairs and necessary governmental and administrative
functions are now being retarded by existing charter pro-
visions concerning its fiscal year, and the welfare of the public
thus affected , an emergency is hereby declared to exist and
this Act shall become effective immediately upon its passage
and approval by the Governor .
Passed and enrolled March 8, approved March 14 , 1946 .
All

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 92.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, relating to the fiscal year of cities other than the first class.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 92.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to establish the fiscal year for cities other than the first class. It specifies that cities of the second class begin their fiscal year on January 1st, with the option for the legislative body to designate June 1st. Cities of the third class begin on June 1st, and cities of the fourth, fifth, and sixth classes begin on January 1st. The act also declares an emergency to allow for immediate effectiveness due to the need to adjust fiscal affairs for a city whose classification recently changed.

CHAPTER 87 207
AN ACT relating to swine .
CHAPTER 86
(H. B. 315 )
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 1. Swine for breeding purposes shall not be im-
ported into Kentucky unless they are accompanied by a health
certificate issued by a graduate licensed veterinarian , showing
them to have been immunized by either the serum -alone or the
serum -simultaneous method . The original of such certificate
shall be attached to the waybill covering the shipment , and
a duplicate thereof shall be forwarded to the office of the State
Veterinarian . If the serum -alone method is used , the certifi-
cate must show such treatment to have been administered
within 10 days immediately preceding date of shipment , or
that the serum - simultaneous treatment shall have been ad-
ministered at least 30 days prior to date of shipment .
Sec . 2. Any person who violates section one of this Act
shall be fined not less than two hundred dollars nor more than
one thousand dollars for each offense .
Passed March 15 , enrolled March 18 , approved March 23 , 1946 .

AN ACT repealing , amending and re - enacting Section 94.730 of the Ken-
tucky Revised Statutes, being Chapter 61 , Acts of 1942 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
THAT Section 94.730 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
(being Chapter 61 , Acts of 1942 ) is hereby repealed , amended
and re -enacted , so that as amended and re -enacted , the said
Section shall read as follows :
Whenever in the opinion of the Board of Commissioners
or other legislative body of any city of the second class , land
or other property , or right of way or easement over or through
any property , is needed by the city to construct or erect , or to
permit others , including the United States of America or any
governmental department , branch or agency thereof, to con-
struct or erect a wall or other barrier , for the purpose of pro-
tecting the city and its property and the property and land
of its inhabitants from floods or high water , either within or
beyond the boundaries of the city and in the county in which
the city is located , the city's Board of Commissioners or other
legislative body may , by resolution reciting the need , order
the condemnation of the land or other property , or right of
way or easement , needed for such purpose or project , and may
proceed to condemn and acquire title to any such land or other
property or right of way or easement , as provided by and
pursuant to the provisions of Sections 94.680 to 94.720 , in-
clusive , of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , and as further
provided by and pursuant to the provisions hereinafter set
out :
( 1 ) The judge of the circuit court shall, upon the filing
of a petition by the city , seeking condemnation of any of such
land or other property , or right of way or easement for such
purpose or project , grant and award immediate possession
and full and complete use and control of said land or other

amending Section 435.240 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes by add-
ing a subsection thereto relating to the desertion of such child afte

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
1. That Section 435.240 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes be amended by adding thereto an additional section , to
be known as Subsection 3 , which shall provide as follows :
If a divorce has been granted by a court of competent
jurisdiction , and the parent of any child under the age of
sixteen years shall desert or abandon such child , without com-
plying with the orders of the court as set forth in the judg
ment granting said divorce relative to the custody or support
of said child , or if such parent having custody of such child
under the orders of said court shall abandon and desert said
child , or if a parent , not having custody of said child , shall
take and carry away said child from the custody of the parent
to whom the custody of such child was awarded by said judg-

custody , the parent so doing shall be deemed guilty of a felony

CHAPTER 91
AN ACT relating to Elections .
(H. B. 331 )
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 117.420 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows :
Sec . 1. Members of county boards of registration and
purgation shall receive from three to eight dollars each per
day , the exact compensation per day to be fixed by the fiscal
court, for not more than twenty days per year , except in
counties containing cities of the first class , in which counties
each member shall receive $ 1,200 per annum , to be paid by the
fiscal court in monthly installments . In counties that do not
have a city of the first class , the board shall meet for not more
than fifteen days before any primary election and for not
more than fifteen days before any regular election . In counties
having a city of the first class , the board may meet at any
time necessary to perform its duties , and shall remain in ses-
sion for the purpose of hearing challenges continuously from
the date that the registration books are closed before any
primary or regular election . In all counties the board shall
hold all of its meetings at the courthouse unless it is necessary
that it hold meetings at some other place in the county , but
no meeting shall be held at any other place in the county un-
less the persons affected by the meeting are given written
notice of its time and place .
Sec . 2. Subsection ( 2 ) of section 117.430 of the Ken-
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows :
Each county board of registration and purgation shall
employ the purgation officers provided for in KRS Chapter
117. Purgation officers shall receive from three to eight dol-
lars per day , the exact compensation per day to be fixed by

classification.summary: This act empowers counties to acquire land for permanent public forests, requiring them to be planted with trees within ten years if not already forested. Revenue generated from these forests is to be used for upkeep, acquisition of additional land, and other county purposes as determined by the Fiscal Court. The lands are to be protected from fire and grazing, managed for timber perpetuation, and open to the public for recreational purposes as long as it doesn't interfere with forest use. The Fiscal Court is responsible for the supervision and care of these forests.

title to any real property within such county for any ad-

for said real property .

220 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 97
(H. B. 354 )
AN ACT amending Section 132.630 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes ,
relating to compensation of the County Tax Commissioner and
deputies in counties containing a city of the first class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 132.630 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes of
1944 is amended to read as follows :
(1 ) The county tax commissioner in counties containing
a city of the first class shall annually , in January , report to
the Department of Finance under oath the amount received
by him on account of his official duties or position from all
sources during the preceding year, and the amount paid out
by him for deputies or assistants , giving the amounts paid
to each and for expenses of his office . If it appears from
this statement that he received as compensation on account
of his office , from all sources , more than five thousand dol-
lars after the payment of his deputies and assistants and all
the expenses of his office , he shall , with such statement , pay
to the Department of Finance the amount so received in ex-
cess of five thousand dollars .
( 2 ) The annual salary of the deputy county tax com-
missioners , appraisers and draftsmen shall be fixed by the
county judge by an order entered of record . The salary of
the chief deputy shall not exceed $ 3,300 per annum , that of
the first three deputies shall not exceed $ 2,750 per annum ,
that of the twelve appraisers shall not exceed $ 2,500 per an-
num , and that of the other deputies and draftsmen shall not
exceed $2,200 per annum . The total amount allowed for
salaries for the county tax commissioner , his deputies , ap-
praisers and draftsmen , and all other expenses of the office
including transportation expense for himself and deputies ,
shall not exceed in the aggregate the compensation allowed
by KRS 132.590 . A copy of the order of the county judge
fixing the salaries as well as a copy of any change made
therein , shall be filed with the Department of Revenue by the
county clerk when made .
Passed March 20 , enrolled March 21 , approved March 26 , 1946 .

CHAPTER 99 223
Sub -section ( 3 ) of Section 302.080 , Kentucky Revised Statutes ; to
repeal Sections 298.240 to 298.300 , inclusive , Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes, and to repeal all laws or parts of laws in conflict with this Act
to the extent of such conflict .
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Sec . 1- PURPOSE OF ACT
The purpose of this Act is to promote the public welfare
by regulating insurance rates to the end that they shall be
reasonable , adequate and not unfairly discriminatory , and
to authorize cooperative action among insurers in rate mak-
ing , and in other matters within the scope of this Act . Noth-
ing in this Act is intended ( 1 ) to prohibit or discourage
reasonable competition , or ( 2 ) to prohibit , or encourage ex-
cept to the extent necessary to accomplish the aforemention-
ed purpose , uniformity in insurance rates , rating plans or
practices . This Act shall be liberally interpreted to carry
into effect the provisions of this Section .
Sec . 2- SCOPE OF ACT
This Act applies to all kinds , types or forms of insurance
written by fire , marine , and fire and marine insurance com-
panies on risks located in this state , including inland marine
insurance and all other kinds , types , or forms of motor
vehicle insurance , and applies with respect to such kinds ,
types or forms of insurance to inter -insurers or reciprocal
underwriters regulated by Sections 302.080 to 302.140 , in-
clusive , of the Kentucky Revised Statutes , and mutual in-
surers regulated by Sections 301.010 to 301.140 , inclusive , of
the Kentucky Revised Statutes , and insurers operating on
the Lloyd's Plan , regulated by Sections 302.150 to 302.230 , in-
clusive , of the Kentucky Revised Statutes .
This Act shall not apply
(a) to reinsurance ;
(b) to insurance of vessels or craft , their cargoes ,
marine builders ' risks , marine protection and indemnity, or
other risks commonly insured under marine , as distinguished
from inland marine insurance policies ;
(c) to insurance of hulls of aircraft , including acces-
sories and equipment , and insurance against liability for
damage to property or injury to persons arising out of the
ownership , maintenance or use of aircraft .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1946, p. 240

form of insurance , and shall thereafter remain subject to this
Act in all particulars as to any such kind , type or form of in-
surance for which such election has been made .
Sec . 3- RATE MAKING
Rates shall be made in accordance with the following
provisions :
(a ) Manual , minimum , class rates , rating schedules or
rating plans , shall be made and adopted , except in the case
of specific inland marine rates on risks specially rated .
(b ) Rates shall be reasonable , adequate and not unfair-
ly discriminatory .
(c ) Consideration shall be given to past and prospec-
tive loss experience both within and outside , the State , in-
cluding the conflagration and catastrophe hazards , to all
other relevant factors within and outside the State , to the
loss trend , to a reasonable underwriting profit, and in the
case of participating insurers to dividends , savings or unab-
sorbed premium deposits allowed or returned by insurers to
their policyholders , members or subscribers , and in the case
of fire insurance rates other than motor vehicles considera-
tion shall be given to the experience of the fire insurance
business during a period of not less than the most recent
five year period for which such experience is available .
(d) Every authorized insurer shall file on call by the
Director , but not more frequently than once each year with
